lines.st: All shall return to fire.
While you, my unruly shadow—you had every opportunity to avoid involving yourself in this.
But since you decided to jump into the flames together,
Come.
Join this magnificent blaze where even shadows lose themselves.

lines.x: So we're basically right outside Londinium?
That's what I heard.
I've never walked so much my entire life.
You figure you've walked more than Moran and the others?
Them? They followed the Leader all the way from the Scáthanna Fields, how can I even compare?
Well, who's counting? Besides, we're here. What now? We fight?
I suppose. We've been through more than our fair share of scuffles on our way here anyway—stray Sarkaz, wasteland bandits, soldiers hired by nobles...
Sorry, present company excluded. I meant the ones who specifically target Tarans, you know the type.
I know. I just wanted to say...
No, it's nothing.
Come on, out with it. Is your leg better now? Can you do a little jig?
The slightly sultry breeze of early autumn blows the stench of swamp and grass into the air.
The soldier separated from his squad looks up towards Londinium. His ears are not being deafened by sporadic cannonfire, nor is the Sarkaz witchcraft he heard so much about anywhere to be seen.
Fine, I'll try. Just don't step on my leg.
They're dancing in the camp? I'll tell them to stop. We can't risk the noise this close to the battlefield.
No, it's fine. We'll be safe as long as we're inside the camp.
But we have to press forward.
The road ahead passes through a battlefield, and I should go alone.
Reed! Moran! Come dance with us!
...So you're all here.
Battlefield's just up ahead. No clue how we're gonna end up...
But everyone's in pretty good shape for now, so let's dance while we still can!
Maybe if we move enough, it'll shake off the fear—
That's lovely, but... I'd rather everyone stay here and go no further.
Huh? We're not going to battle?
It's too dangerous for you.
So you're going on ahead, all by your lonesome?
I'm sorry.
How long will you be gone?
I don't know. But I'll definitely return.
Reed, everyone who followed you here knows that fighting is inevitable.
We all know we're not qualified to join the fray. But we want to help you, even if all we can do is to block a few bolts.
I'm not qualified either.
...Reed?
Be it Wellington, the other Dukes, or the Sarkaz, they are the ones tipping the scales of this war. But us...
No matter which side we choose, we'll make less of a difference on the battlefield than a speck of dust. That goes for both you and me.
Then why...?
There is something I must do.
But don't mistake me. What I intend is no nobler than facing off with the Victorians for our people, swords drawn. I just... have to face it...
To face her.
'The Leader'?
The Leader.

lines.st: Almost every Taran knows of Dublinn and 'The Leader'.
Even those not in the know will soon learn of their existence from the increased Victorian aggression after Wellington's army marched off towards Londinium.
Of course, they will also realize that the Leader of Dublinn standing before them would seem out of place with the fabled 'spectre force', or on Wellington's high-speed battleship.
But she saved them and asked nothing of them, so to them she is without a doubt The Leader.

lines.x: People come and go. Those of us still here have already made up our minds, so don't you worry on our behalf.
Don't mind my wounds. I still have a chance against the restless dead.
You saved my life. Even if I die here, it'll be a worthy death.
Thank you, everyone. But we didn't come all this way to become martyrs.
The road ahead of us doesn't lead to yet more fighting. On the contrary, when I return, we'll... return to a Tara without war. Together.
So you're coming back for sure, Reed?
I am.
Then we'll wait for you.
Thank you.
After the battle at Silverrock Bluffs, the Royal Court forces retreated behind Londinium's walls, while Victoria's Dukes kept their forces stationed around the city, in no hurry to assault it.

Wellington's army, with their fresh new flag, did not participate in the siege, and stationed themselves just outside the warzone like an indifferent observer, coolly watching the deadlock outside the city.
But a stalemate does not necessarily mean peace. Scouting efforts and countermeasures, raids and ambushes, Sarkaz witchcraft and brief cannon barrages...
Accompanying them are the curses and groans of the immobilized wounded, unable to do anything but wait in vain for death to come. Everything comes together in a tapestry of stifling tranquility.
The Draco takes advantage of the current 'peace' and sets off to put an end to it.
How's the situation outside the city?
A group of Victorians tried to snatch their wounded off the battlefield a few hours ago, but we bled them dry.
But... they'll beat us to death if they spot any of us alone, and their firepower's terrifying to boot.
Captain, is it true? What that so-called Babel on the radio said the other day—
Shut up if you want to live!
What? A Vouivre walking around outside the city? Even helping to treat the wounded? Is she mocking us? Does she think we're already as good as dead?
I'll give you a minute. Consider your lives forfeit if she's still breathing.
Wait, what? You're telling me she's even saving the Sarkaz?
Bloody devils... They've been holed up in there for days and still pack a punch...
Personally, I'd rather the siege continue.
Old Wellington's not in the siege. Changed flags and retreated to his own camp. It's obvious he no longer wants to fight the Sarkaz.
Her Grace the Duke of Caster, in her magnanimity, has ordered us to not pursue the matter any further, but where are we going to find more unlucky blokes to hold off the Sarkaz troops?
I mean, I'm certainly not volunteering for the duty.
Are there even any Victorians left in the city, what with so many wounded soldiers out here on the field?
Reporting in. We have... returned.
What?! How?! Everyone we sent to rescue you never came back!
A Vouivre... a Caster, she saved us.
A Vouivre? Which Duke does she serve? Gododdin? Fife?
No idea. She didn't even seem to care which side we were on.
Sod it, just who was she? You're saying she was going, what, towards the city?
Seemed like... to the Gastrell.
I'm sorry, this is all I can do for you.
It's okay. I'm just relieved... my chest doesn't hurt anymore, at least.
Thank you so much, Leader. I've seen you before, at Oak Grove... You said then, 'The flames have already been kindled.'
So now I'll become one of the restless dead, and continue fighting for Tara even after I die, right?
Is that what you want?
Then my answer is this: You won't.
What...?
The flames will indeed swallow up many lives, but never should they intentionally use life as tinder. That isn't how flame is meant to be used.
Do you know the legend of the watchmen? Those who send returning Taran warriors to sleep in flame, so they may finally know rest.
Your fight is over, soldier. I hope you find rest just as they did.
Leader, I can't...
This is an order.
...As you command.
Brigadier, sir.
What's the situation with our target?
She's been treating any wounded on her way, but otherwise has been headed straight towards our camp. She'll soon reach the Gastrell's gangway.
What of the Sarkaz and the other Dukes' movements?
The Sarkaz have made a few probing attacks with small squads, but are evidently unaware of the situation and have failed to slow her down.
Continue surveillance, and prepare for her to board. Eliminate any threat to her life.
Yes, sir.
Another thing, Brigadier, sir. We and you Tarans... have already become companions on the battlefield after risking our lives together, yes?
No need to beat around the bush. Ask me any question you have.
I've heard some rumors regarding the target. That you once planned to get rid of her.
So why are you protecting her now?
Reporting in. Target is at the gangway!
(Inquiring look)
Let her board. Bring her to the command center.
(Mouthing) The bridge?!
...Let her board, and bring her to the Gastrell's command center.
Yes, sir!
I thought... you would have welcomed her personally.
Me?
I'm no longer qualified.
Your Grace?
I thought the one waiting for me... would be my sister.
It is inconvenient for Princess Eblana to meet you at the moment, Miss Loughshinny.
But I am quite interested in how you managed to obtain the G-0 high ground coordinates. Is it the pharmaceutical company you stay at? That Doctor?
...Yes.
Then, milady, would you kindly pass along a message? The Doctor will have to personally come and get whatever is wanted from Tara.
The Doctor was only helping me meet my sister, expecting nothing from Tara. Only hoping to end the war sooner. And I agree.
Pardon my frankness, Miss Loughshinny, but I see only a mouthpiece here, and I believe mouthpieces have no authority to make suggestions.
I am no go-between. I represent only myself.
Then I shall hear you out.
What exactly do you want?
Peace.
Peace?
The cannons still ring, the Sarkaz witchcraft still hovers above our heads, and you speak of peace?
It is only because I am meeting you in good faith that I will choose not to regard that as an insult to this war.
That is absolutely not my intention.
This war continues not because it cannot be ended, Your Grace. Even I can tell the Sarkaz are running out of time.
The Victorians have surrounded the city but have refrained from an assault, in order to preserve their strength.
I suppose they plan to starve out the city.
They do not care if any of their own countrymen remain inside. It does not matter if there actually are, so long as they don't see a single one when they eventually enter the city.
An ugly tactic, if there ever was one.
The root of ugliness is cowardice, as it always has been.
The morality of one's actions does not diminish its cowardice, Miss Loughshinny. Cowardice is when you hesitate to take responsibility.
...Which is why I am standing before you now.
I hope that you and your forces can put an end to this ugly siege, break the Sarkaz's fragile front, and spare every Victorian in the city from the calamity created by their own people.
I see. Now I understand why you have come.

As Her Highness Eblana has said, you show both compassion and nobility. But these virtues of yours are far from a reason for the Tarans to once again involve themselves in a conflict between the Sarkaz and the Victorians.
The Gastrell would not be stationed here if intervention was never an option.
I asked for a reason, Miss Loughshinny.
I, the Gastrell, and the countless other Taran warriors require a reason good enough for us to risk our own lives.
Please, Your Grace.
'Please' has no power on the battlefield.
Reed tries her best to look straight into Wellington's eyes.
She knows well what holds the most power on the battlefield. She knows she is qualified to wield it—and that that right is all she has.
She also knows that once she uses it, there will be no turning back.
But she was never uninvolved, and that is precisely why she came here.
(Takes a deep breath)
Then, Your Grace, I... I order... you.
In the name of Tara.
I order you to rescue the Victorian civilians inside the city.
I order your fleet to smash the weak Sarkaz defense line, to bring Londinium the relief she should have had long ago.
I order the Taran army to demonstrate noblesse oblige, not for material benefit, but for all to see our glory.
As you command.
Any other orders?
You've been stationed outside Londinium this whole time, yet you acquiesced to my command so easily... Could it be because my sister...?
No.
My presence here means only one thing: that once again, I was able to witness the cowardice and evil of the Victorians.
As long as you are willing to give orders, you will have command.
You merely made a choice. To align yourself with Her Highness Eblana, and the newborn Tara.
...I did.
I would have come here even if the city was not being besieged.
If so, then I believe Her Highness will soon find the time to grant you an audience. I'm sorry to say, but this truly is an inconvenient time for her to meet you.
What is she planning?
Does it matter? Whether it succeeds or fails, it will nonetheless benefit Tara.
...Truly?
She made a promise. And she will honor it.
Do excuse me.
The command center returns to silence as Reed scrutinizes the conversation they just had.
She completed some of her objectives and learned a few things, but is left even more puzzled.
She has stepped in, and can no longer turn back.
Miss Loughshinny.
You are...?
The general ordered me to escort you out and show you to your cabin.
You may spend your time aboard the Gastrell as you please before we reach Taran territory.
My apologies, but there are people waiting for me.
Do you mean the Tarans who have been following you?
The general did consider this. He is willing to send a squad to lead them to our camp here, and let them board a ride on a support ship back to the capital, Na Saoirsí.
No, I promised them I would return.
Just as I am promising you right now, and have promised His Grace, that I will return to Na Saoirsí.
I believe we made that clear in our earlier exchange.
My apologies, but I'll need to ask the general about this...
He's busy? Ugh... But...
What?! With your busy schedule, even...
Y—Yes! As you command!
Miss Loughshinny.
I'm listening.
Direct orders from The Leader. She has instructed us not to interfere with your movements.
'The Leader'?
Yes.
But she does ardently hope that you can stay just a little while longer.
To witness a grand performance.
Are you sure about not meeting her personally?
No need to.
Strictly speaking, the war has yet to end. Nothing good will come of meeting Loughshinny now. It would only bring her more confusion.
Very well, Your Highness. Please return to the command center and await the good news.
Hold on.
What do you mean?
Seeing that Loughshinny is willing to tie her own fate to Tara's, we should show her the might of the nation she is about to be a part of.
Rest assured. We would demonstrate it with the Sarkaz's prompt defeat.
But if you prefer a more personal show of force, the Gastrell is more than willing to oblige.
Consider it a grand welcome.
As my dear sister has made her wishes clear, shouldn't I help her realize them with my own hands?
Didn't I tell you it doesn't matter where that Vouivre came from? She's helping the Victorians, so she's got to die. Simple as that.
We're... We're no match for her.
You're worthless!
...Bah, forget it. It's just one Vouivre—
What was that noise?
A—A high-speed battleship! Currently speeding towards the city walls!
Victorian?
It's the Gastrell—it's that Duke of Wellington guy!
Not just him, Caster's fleet seems to be spontaneously coordinating with his ships!
Raid! Raid incoming—
Purple flames?! Captain—
Atop the Gastrell's bow stands The Leader of Dublinn, purple flames spreading from the tip of her spear to the city walls.
Her flames of death erupt from the Sarkaz on the perimeter, spreading to adjacent victims as they wantonly seek the next host, leaving only the floating Nachzehrer untouched by fire.
Nachzehrer. 'God of War'? 'Lord of War and Death'?
A shame there is no longer enough death here to sate you.
Even living legends have their endings. But surely becoming the kindling of a new legend is better than wasting away in obscurity?
Oh. The brazenly ambitious young Draco.
Very well then.
Red Dragon who toys with life and death on a spear. Just how well do you comprehend death?
I suppose this is not the worst way to kill time before the war is over.
Do try to hold on a little longer, Nachzehrer.
Let us show those Victorians what death truly is, while the artillery still rings.
I have waited a long time, young Draco.
You are bolder than the others.
Then use those flames of yours to send a message to those cowards surrounding the city—
I am here.
The war is here.
The stench of rot thickens as the ice-cold flames of death rage.
Death crashes into death, over the faint shelling and thin city walls.
Th—They really started fighting?
Did The Leader...
Shush! There's no way she'd...
No. We have to go find her.
We promised we'd wait for her here.
But the fighting's broken out in Londinium again!
...Just believe in her—
I do, but listen! That's the sound of witchcraft, and the Dukes' cannons! How could The Leader—
—Like she believes in us.
The wounded soldier is left speechless, only able to look back on the tranquility now torn apart by flame and chaos. Searching for a reason for his squad to join the battlefield.
Past the smoke and mire he sees a figure.
The Leader. Reed. Loughshinny.
She finished her own fight, held true to her promise, and returns to lead them on, to the end of their journey...
To home, to peace.
Let's go, everyone.
What is happening there is no longer something we can involve ourselves in.
It's time to head home.
Here's the files on Chernobog, chief. They're on your desk.
Hope you can find the info you wanted somewhere in those dusty papers.
Thanks for sorting them out, Zhenya.
The assistant closes the door as he leaves, as usual. It scrapes against the uneven flooring, letting out a shrieking sound.
The editor sits up straight, lights a cigarette, and pushes through the discomfort as he flips through the stack of old newspapers.
'The Third Army's Dereliction of Duty in the Chernobog Incident...'
Tch... Same old, same old. 'Chernobog Closes Mine—a Heavy Blow to Ursus's Originium Industry'.
...'Novochernobog—The Infected Lives a Blessed Life Under His Majesty's Glory'.
Utter drivel.
No, this isn't what I want... These trashy tabloids are barely scratching the surface.
...None of these reports are anywhere close to the truth.
The editor pushes up his glasses and flicks away some stray cigarette ash, but accidentally sends sparks flying onto the paper.
He rushes to extinguish the sudden fire, but the newspaper already bears a freshly burnt hole.
Through the hole the editor sees an ensemble of letters—'Reunion'.
A name he has never heard of before.
In the last few days, a stream of Infected have been showing up wanting to join Reunion.
I told them we'll be leaving Londinium in one month's time, heading for the Duke of Gododdin's territory.
Are they willing to follow us there?
They neither agreed nor refused. They couldn't come up with an answer.
...I'll prepare for bringing them along. They have nowhere else to go.
Gododdin. I hear the Duke's issued more than a few decrees benefiting the Infected, so it's worth checking out.
Decrees and reality are not the same thing.
Obviously. Which is why we need to see for ourselves.
From a corner comes a rustling sound as Ch'en turns a newspaper page.
Nothing to say even after listening in for so long, Ch'en?
Everything you've talked about is internal business.
As an outsider, I have no right to comment. I've been focusing on the news anyway.
You weren't this quiet with many of the others in private.
We need your thoughts, Hui-chieh.
When I let you tag along with us, it wasn't just to show you how different we are from the other organizations.
Don't go getting the idea that you'd have to take responsibility for either of us.
Given our current situation, anyone's input is welcome, as long as we can help these people out. So speak your mind.
You're planning to take in people with nowhere else to go?
Why not?
They'll bring new vitality to our group.
Or new conflicts.
This group you've gathered under the banner of what you're calling 'Reunion' is as rag-tag as they come.
Is this what you promised me when you left?
Not to mention her.
Ch'en looks toward the indifferent Talulah.
You're bound to attract more eyes in future.
So if you ask me, I suggest staying in Londinium for a while, and restructuring yourselves—
You mean letting someone more suitable handle it?
I won't deny that.
Just look at the people you have here. Criminals, Infected, deserters... Do you have a clue where you're taking them?
We're not going to stay here forever, at the very least.
What is it? What's the hurry?
Nine, Red just came back with some urgent news. Ugh—
He shoots a glance at Ch'en.
Ever since the stranger called Ch'en showed up, his companions have been behaving strangely.
She rarely leaves Talulah and Nine alone, but has never once commented on Reunion's actions either.
Go on. She's one of us.
Troops from the ducal alliance are approaching the factory. Should we get them to leave?
Ducal troops? They can't be coming for us, but... I'll head there myself and see what's going on.
Talulah... keep everyone else hidden.
Alright.
So, you agree with Nine's methods?
...Not exactly. But this group is nothing like the original group I traveled with on the tundra.
Unfortunately, oppressed peoples are treated the same way no matter where they are.
No one cares where we go, Hui-chieh, only where we decide to stop. And then they'll figure out how to drive us away.
'Reunion'. It's a mistake I've made myself...
Nine has no intention of wiping my slate clean, but if she needs me to come up with a new meaning—
I have no right to refuse.
And her conscience is crystal clear.
Nine... and myself, we know very well what we're doing.
But you, Hui-chieh. You chased me—or rather, Reunion—all the way here.
Why are you still delaying what you decided long ago?
Talulah leaves. Ch'en doesn't catch up with her.
Keep moving, you beasts. Don't stop!
Tch... Bloody bastards!
Get up! Now!
Look at these disgusting sick freaks. Wish I could just get rid of them here and now.
The Exemplars' territory is just ahead. Are you considering dumping our rubbish right on their doorstep?
I say we stay out of trouble. They've already set up garbage disposal stations, so no littering.
Garbage disposal...?
I won't ask when or where you got your orders, soldier.
But this is no place for an execution.
Ah, the Infected hiding in the factory.
You should be thankful that we're busy catching these devils instead of—
The air grows warm. Nine casts a quick glance toward a corner shrouded in darkness.
Within it, a fire ignites, then slowly dims.
You... Did you just feel a tingling on your skin, like—
Like I got burned by fire.
...Itching for a fight, huh?
This factory area is under the Exemplars' protection.
So why don't you go ask your superiors if they'd mind an idiotic subordinate stirring up conflict here and smashing this hard-won peace to pieces?
I think you'll know how to speak to us after that.
But suppose our allies, our 'friends', were to show inappropriate sympathy and compassion towards our common enemy—
I imagine my superiors would be humming a different tune.
Move, Infected.
...They don't sound like they came from Kazdel.
Devils are freakishly good at disguises.
There's no way we're letting a devil merc slide past us.
Get moving, you damned devils!
Ugh...
The squad escorting the Sarkaz slowly trudge through the long street, though both captors and captives look exhausted and numb.
Nine watches as they leave, and spots a few teenagers stumbling at the end of the downcast parade of prisoners.
Bastards...
Here's the place, sir. The blaze lasted for three days and left basically nothing behind.
Did the fire spread anywhere other than the Patrol Unit's outpost?
No... but many miners seized the chance to escape. Everyone was so scared... Didn't know when the Patrol Unit would arrive...
But seems like you got here first. You said you're a reporter? But who's to say for sure?
...Let's not get into that. So, if the management here has always been hard on everyone, how did the miners carry out the arson?
The miners?
No, no. The village had an old noble from the city, good heart, that man... but a bit too kindhearted, if you ask me.
Couldn't stand what was happening in the mines, always talking about rescuing the miners. We thought it was all talk, but in the end...
He really went and did it.
Oh? Then where is he now?
Must've died in the blaze with the Patrol Unit.
Hah. A noble who bravely sacrificed his life to help the suffering Infected miners.
That would make a pretty good headline.
The reporter gazes at the scorched land. Nearby buildings have been reduced to rubble, and in place of the once lush forest stands unsightly tree stumps.
Suddenly, a scorch mark draws his attention. It looks unusual, almost aesthetically pleasing.
Do you... know who left this mark here?
That's just from the fire.
I'm not buying that. It looks like a 'symbol' to me.
So—who left it here?
Uh... no clue. It was just here after the fire.
So you're saying it was a deliberate act, an attempt to send out a signal: 'Salvation'.
A-Am I?
(Laughs) Didn't you just explain the real history behind it for me?
No need to rush. Get in line, everyone will get some.
How much longer do we have to stay here, Miss Ch'en?
When will the next squad leave the city...?
Don't worry. You're safe here.
I've reached out through many different channels to get everyone out of here.
I-I heard the Sarkaz-hunting soldiers have found this place.
If they find out you're sheltering us...
Sheltering? Why speak as if you're fugitives on the run?
You're citizens of Londinium, victims of this war.
We were already unwelcome long before the Sarkaz troops entered the city.
Believe me, it's more than just soldiers on that team.
Being a Sarkaz is already a crime, the worst one you can commit. Doesn't matter if you took part in the war or not.
...I know.
Anyways... Thanks for taking us in.
I ironed it out with the others. We'll wait three days for a chance to leave the city. If we don't get one, we'll go by ourselves.
It's not the best time to leave. The soldiers have been even more active recently.
All the more reason to get out as soon as we can. Every day we put it off will only worsen the situation.
...If you insist, we'll help you out.
Appreciate the generosity. I suggest you guys do the same.
Who do you think they'll target next, once the city's out of Sarkaz?
Most of you are Infected. You should know how their nobles treat you guys.
Today's paper, sir.
Hmm... I can't be bothered to read it, Alyosha. Anything noteworthy?
New tax policies, some literary works, a few scandals, and...
And?
Revolts. All over the country.
Let me see.
Finally, some real reporting. I thought those Deity Grypherburg reporters only knew how to run tabloids.

'Infected have been striking at factories and outposts across the country. Some regions are experiencing a surge of uprisings, as Infected have brazenly attacked outposts and government agencies, causing serious losses...'
'It is said that they are inspired by an Infected organization known as Reunion...'
Oh, look, they even left some symbols...
...Do you wish to end your convalescence now?
Of course not. Do you even realize how hard it is for me to get a vacation?
But your colleagues from the propaganda department have visited you many times already.
Oh, my dear Alyoshenka, our dear nation has been suffering from a chronic disease for quite some time, and there are many who wish to heal her.
But to do so, these rotten old wounds must first see the light of day.
I've had enough of my colleagues patching them up, over and over.
How should I reply then?
Tell them any discussion must wait until my vacation is over.
Did they ransack the streets again for another batch of prisoners? Here's your soup, Talulah.
Thanks.
Too bland? Well—I had to ransack our stash to get the last of the salt.
It's fine. Good, even.
Heh. 'Ransack'? That's a good word.
Only a week ago they wouldn't have come here, let alone taken any children among the prisoners.
But we just saw both happen.
They've been dead set on wiping out any trace of the Sarkaz for the past two weeks.
At first, it was just Sarkaz soldiers who couldn't evacuate in time. Next, it was any Sarkaz civilian who had any dealings with the army. Now? Even Londinium-born Sarkaz aren't being spared.
I thought you'd make a move, Hui-chieh.
...That would only complicate things. We're in Victoria, so I have to act more cautiously.
That'll only cost us more time.
A few days ago I saw a group of kids harassing a poor woman... all she did was to marry a Sarkaz and raise their children together.
This is how they treated the Infected before the war, and now it's the Sarkaz's turn. Eventually, every Londinium citizen will face this.
Time is what these mistreated people lack most.
Nine pauses to glance at Talulah right beside her.
However, she seems unusually calm, as if the sudden temperature increase earlier was just a hallucination.
I thought you'd make a move on the Victorians earlier.
It was only a warning. It'd only bring us more trouble if we pestered them any further. Hui-chieh has a point—We should keep a low profile until we're forced to show ourselves.
Attacking ducal alliance troops on the factory streets certainly won't do anything to improve the lives of the workers here.
So you weren't angry.
What you're experiencing, I have too, long ago. But at least Sarkaz blood isn't contagious like Oripathy.
I've long since learned to control my rage. At the very least, I won't waste it on clueless underlings like the soldiers from before.
This war has turned the oppressed into oppressors. It's not their fault.
Then who should the innocent Sarkaz vent their anger on?
Remember that Sarkaz merchant with the motorbike who helped us get supplies...? The Infected killed him out of fear.
Now, that fear is spreading unchecked as the Sarkaz are gradually retreating.
Infected, soldiers, nobles... heh, even civilians who've never seen war. Everyone thinks they're in the right.
So you're correct, Talulah. Sarkaz blood isn't contagious like Oripathy... but hatred and fear are.
You've decided then?
To save the Sarkaz? I see no need to hesitate. They're no different from us.
I'm just not sure how to minimize the fallout, so I need your help.
Keep the people at camp safe, as well as the workers.
...If you need my flames to burn for them, I won't hesitate.
Red, let the workers know our plan. Those willing to join us can stay; anyone too afraid to escape, bring them to the Exemplars' camp.
Got it.
Ch'en.
You can try to stop us, or you can leave and pretend nothing happened.
Saving those Sarkaz means you'll have to change everything you've planned.
And if you go with them, none of the Dukes will let you stay long in their domain, Nine.
They won't let us stay long anyway, even without those people.
We're Infected, Ch'en. Maybe your time at Rhodes Island has made you forget how we're actually treated.
Then where will you go next?
So you agree with our plan?
Even prisoners shouldn't be executed so hastily. They don't even have an official order.
Hui-chieh, the issue isn't where Nine and the others want to go, but where they're needed.
We'll find a place that truly needs us, Ch'en.
Not just the Infected... Anyone enslaved or deprived of their right to a fair chance at survival.
A place with no discrimination or oppression.
...You know as well as the rest of us that that'll be difficult, if not impossible, Nine.
Of course. But this is the path a man we all knew left us before he died.
Guard...
Someone has to see it through, Hui-chieh.
This new Reunion makes their own medicine and strives to help anyone in need, without asking for anything tangible in return.
Change is happening, but it's too fragile.
They need something to believe in, an objective. That's the only way they can push forward.
I don't completely agree with Nine's way of doing things, but I see no reason to refuse to cooperate either.
She's always admired the 'fairness' you seek, Hui-chieh.
So why not take a look at what she seeks?
On the tundra ignored by the Emperor's edict, rebel activity has intensified, with thousands of Infected banding together to break their shackles and regain their freedom.
We've achieved what used to be unthinkable.
Ever since that first fire ignited by that anonymous noble, many of us have been communicating in hushed tones: Is there someone leading us? To be honest, I'm not too sure myself.
What I do know is that our call to rise up has not been silenced, and is even now trying to escape from the tundra and its army of trees blocking the way.
Our proof is that unique symbol everyone's curious about, the symbol scorched by flame.
The hoarse voice on the radio smashes the countryside's peace apart.
People open their doors or climb onto their windowsills, listening quietly.
The broadcast excites them, their palms feeling hot even though they live in a place with temperatures far below zero.
Then, they light up torches in the center of the village. The light illuminates their eyes, and warms the bodies underneath their thin layers of clothing.
Soon, nearby villages spot the firelight and start bonfires one after the other.
On the dark tundra, countless fires light up the night sky.
We gather on this inhospitable tundra, following in their footsteps to take root here.
Everyone has heard of the group that disappeared into Chernobog to fight for the Infected: 'Reunion'.
Have they heard our call?
Have they finally returned—
Alrighty, you dregs! Turn around!
Sir... please... spare my life, please...
I was born here!
I-I'm different from the rest, sir, please!
I said, shut it!
S-Sir, please...
Kid, he doesn't give a damn who you are. He just wants to kill every Sarkaz he sees.
Shut up!
The boy suddenly smells a strange scent. It makes him recall his mother's perfume as she hugged him.
Countless flowers bloom on the bloodstained floors, and the scent grows stronger.
The executioner suddenly coughs violently until he throws up a cluster of bloody flower buds.
Ugh... *cough* *cough*...
Y-You...
Get them out of here. Our people will know what to do.
You lot... don't... *cough*...
Nine! Stop your Originium Arts!
You already saved the Sarkaz! Killing them... is a step too far.
Couldn't you have just kept silent as usual, Ch'en?
Stop making more enemies everywhere you go, unless you want the Dukes chasing after Reunion once you leave the city.
Talulah can make sure that it looks like an accidental blaze.
No. They'll only put the blame on the Sarkaz, calling it an act of revenge.
And then what? Who will the Dukes turn their gaze to? More innocent Sarkaz.
Or, say, an Infected group wandering around Victoria with who-knows-how-many Sarkaz in tow.
Letting them go will only make things worse.
Tell me, Ch'en. When we finally come face to face with the ducal alliance with no chance at escape, how will you keep the Infected and innocent Sarkaz there safe?
Ugh...
Enough! There're still other ways!
The tip of Ch'en's sword touches Nine's throat.
I've imagined this happening countless times.
In fact, I'm shocked it didn't happen sooner.
I went to school with many of their superior officers. I can talk to them.
As what? As a former senior inspector of the L.G.D.? As Operator Ch'en of Rhodes Island? Or, what... an old classmate, reminiscing about the good old days?
...I can guarantee these Sarkaz will be safe, at least.
Of course, I trust you'll keep that promise.
But you're missing my point, Ch'en.
There are limits to one person's power. Even you can't help the people you can't see.
I know your capabilities all too well, and that's exactly why I'm even more sure that your momentary kindness won't change a thing. It can't.
What I need is people willing to follow us, to see with their own eyes what we have to do.
Spare me the sophistry, Nine.
I'm only looking at the facts.
'Sophistry', huh?
Maybe we're all seeking 'fairness', but our interpretations of it are different.
...This is why I've long since given up hope of you coming to understand me and the future I'm looking forward to.
You can most certainly try to arrest me, Officer Ch'en.
Chi Xiao stays in place, and Nine's gaze remains on Ch'en.
A flaming sword shakes Chi Xiao away.
This isn't the place to wax nostalgic about your old days on the force.
Talulah stabs her longsword into the ground, separating the all-tensed-up two.
I didn't call for your help, Talulah. Don't act like you know everything better.
Nine, there is nothing wrong with Hui-chieh's idea.
The Dukes would be overjoyed at someone volunteering to remove every last Sarkaz from Londinium, and they might not make any moves on us.
But more importantly, there are still eyes on the both of you.
They only care about one thing: where should they go now?
The rescued Sarkaz and the Reunion members following Nine are both terrified. They have no idea what to do next.
Many of them can't understand exactly what it is you want, Nine. They just want to live.
They need time. It might take a while to show them the future you see.
Of course I know that.
I have the patience to wait for my 'sophistry' to bloom into a concrete plan of action.
And will you achieve that with even more sacrifices? How do you decide who?
ENOUGH!
I'm not here to determine which of you is right or wrong. It's not my place to.
But for now, just take them away from here. Take everyone who's willing to leave the city with us.
Hui-chieh might manage to prevent the Victorians from harassing us in the short term, but we'll still have to prepare for the worst.
Are you giving me an order, Talulah?
No, I'm offering my opinion.
But where can you go?
Your identities are too conspicuous.
Is this where you say we should go to Rhodes Island?
Yes. I have to see the both of you judged.
But not right now. Many Infected are still relying on you, and I can't just leave them in the dust.
Hah... Classic Officer Ch'en.
Want my opinion?
Whenever Talulah proactively offers her thoughts to Nine, it often leaves the Phidia with even more doubts towards her.
But Talulah has never fallen behind.
She always walks silently in the center of their group, so inconspicuous that people forget she exists.
Reunion's leader, Kashchey's adopted daughter, and the manipulative mastermind behind the Chernobog disaster.
Talulah, regarded as a hero by Infected who do not know the truth.
Her sword begins to burn, and its flames spread across the floor. They jump and fall, outlining a symbol.
The firelight reflects in the trio's eyes, but their faces still bear some gloom—the mark once represented a dark past.
Within it hides all sorts of crimes and injustices, certain to sting if revealed for all to see.
Ursus.
We can go back to Ursus.
...That's a terrible idea.
What, thinking about your unfinished business over there?
No, I want to right my wrongs.
I've received news from Ursus. The Infected are breaking their shackles and congregating on the tundra again.
They claim to be answering the call of Reunion, and have decided to stand up and fight for the rights and freedoms the Infected and other oppressed deserve.
They were inspired by a lie, but their determination to fight back is certainly real.
So they gave you the courage to recall the good old days?
...It's not only Infected over there. There are students, exiled officers and soldiers, guilt-ridden nobles...
It's exactly the strength you need.
Strength that ignores the differences in race or identity and crushes the fear of disease, united by a common goal.
Besides, it'll take time to leave with this many people... and I think that should be long enough for you to explain Guard's precious dream to those still willing to follow you.
...You don't want to restore Reunion's reputation?
No. I never expected it to hold such a large influence even today.
Even if the Ursus Empire has long since covered up what happened at Chernobog.
The voices craving resistance are like water. They might be burnt and dry up, but they'll eventually return to the land as rain.
Returning to Ursus is easier said than done. There are too many factors involved, and more things for me to confirm.
That's your problem. Not ours.
Talulah...
Even if Reunion can turn a new leaf, representing once again freedom and happiness...
What about you, Talulah? Your sins can't be washed away. Your existence is Reunion's darkest history.
How can you face the pure-white tundra as you are?
I know that I stand here today no longer as a leader, but as a symbol, a flag, or even... a weapon to be wielded.
But I'll take it. I'll accept the shackles you place on me. I've had many different identities—Draco, noble, leader, Infected... None of these matter anymore.
Today I remain only someone who once scattered the seeds of sin.
The wrongs I committed in Ursus, I will repay in Ursus.
Repay... you make it sound so simple.
I will not act as just a flag this time.
Then how?
I'll throw myself into a true blaze and let it all burn, until even the tundra's chill melts away.
I'll go back to Ursus... to truly face my own trial.
And you can be there to witness it, Nine.
Witness the end of Reunion, and the birth of something new from its corpse. That's what you truly want, is it not?
You really don't mind?
The Reunion of my heart died long ago on the tundra... along with my companions.
I'll go with you.
Scared I'll harm more innocents?
Both you and Nine.
After following Reunion for a while, I can confirm that the group has changed somewhat.
For good or bad... I can't say.
I don't want to miss your trial, but I also don't want to jump to any hasty conclusions about whether the path Nine insists on is correct.
From now on, I'll keep an eye on you as Rhodes Island Operator Ch'en.
I need to keep Rhodes Island informed, no matter what happens in Ursus.
Talulah, Nine... I'm responsible for you both.
...Whatever.
Now, you should probably get going. Leave the soldiers to me. I'll catch up after I make sure they're okay.
We didn't say we'd wait for you.
Have you ever? I've always had to catch up on my own.
Hmph.
Onwards to Ursus then.
Onwards to Ursus.
The flames continue to spread slowly from the tip of the blade.
Hot flames lick the bodies piled up in a corner, gently enveloping them.
Amid the light crackling of the fire, Talulah silently prays for the flames to bring them to a brighter and warmer place.
Stay close to me, Ollie!
You've got to stick close if you don't want me carrying you! Grab onto my clothes, okay?
Mummy can't hold your hand right now... my palms are all slippery with sweat! So, grab on tight to my clothes.
(Nods)
Colin! Colin?! Where are you? Is there a way out?
Oh no, oh no! This isn't going to work! We should head back home and hide! Ollie's too little for this!
Colin—
Ah!
Over here! I found a way! The Exemplars are drawing the devils away! If we can avoid the explosions—
'Avoid the explosions'?! Do you remember how Schwall from the grocer tripped and fell on that patch of red glow?
It was only a palm-sized wound, but it bled him dry. Did you forget?
It's like the blood was sucked right out of him... Can you really call that just an 'explosion'?
It's because his wound never healed! We'll be fine as long as we're careful not to get cut or scraped.
The one thing we can't do, is turn back.
The explosion just now sounded too close! I'd rather head back than die halfway without knowing what's happening.
Your grandmother's still in the city—
Going back now would only mean more danger.
You're—
We can't say for sure that all the Sarkaz have left the lockdown zone.
You came from outside? Are you with the Exemplars? Are you here to help us? Please!
...Sorry, I'm not with the Exemplars, and I'm not here to rescue people.
I only came to Londinium to look for someone.
Who could be so important that you'd wear out your shoes running around Londinium at a time like this looking for them?
But if you're not with the Exemplars, then you're just another poor sod like us. Let's go back, Colin...
*sigh*
The man sighs and looks around cautiously.
The optician has returned to Londinium for the first time in many years. But the view he sees now is as if he is looking at the city through a lens in the heat of his workshop, blurred and distorted.
By the time the image clears up, the only thing he can see is chaos. Trying to find an elusive presence in this bedlam will be no easy task, let alone someone whose appearance he does not even know.
Could you show us the way out, good sir? I'll give you everything I have!
If you managed to enter the lockdown zone without a scratch, you must know a safe route. Please...
The gaunt man empties his pockets, takes out a few pieces of bread from a bag, and tries to stuff them into Nowell's hands. The woman also takes out what few coins she has on her.
Nowell does not take them. He shakes his head, knowing that it is a thin line that separates these three people from the exsanguinated corpse by the roadside.
And he stands behind that tiny divide, always a spectator.
He has tried to reach out before, but his hands cannot save everyone.
I'm sorry...
Sir!
Let's go, Colin. It's no use...
There's no other way!
...I'm not with the Exemplars, but I know where their camp is.
I can take you there.
...! Th-Thank you! Thank you so much!
But that's all I can do.
I-It's plenty! We'll go with you!
What about your grandmother, Colin?
We'll come back for her once it's all over. The devils may be cruel, but even they wouldn't hurt the helpless old folks in the Sanatorium...
Right?
I'll lead you out. After that...
You're on your own.
That's plenty, good sir, I couldn't possibly ask for any more. Thank you so much!
...No need to thank me. Grab your child and follow me.
This way.
This path will take you straight to the city walls.
The Exemplars have set up camp around a breach in the wall.
You could also seek refuge with the ducal alliance's army outside.
We...
What about you, sir? I gather you aren't coming with us.
You saved our lives. I don't know how you did it, but if there's anything we can do for you...
No need. My wounds have healed.
Even—even with your unique talents, the cannons won't show you any more mercy than they do us. You should come with us... you're Victorian too.
No. I've... I've come this far.
I have to find the one I'm looking for. I have no reason to stop.
You mentioned you were looking for someone. Who?
This is not the first time she's asked, but it is the first time she hears something other than calmness in his voice.
The numerous wounds the man took, which should have killed him many times over, have all healed. Any bleeding quickly stopped, and even Sarkaz Arts Circles could not affect him.
Only the pain persists. Even so, he clenches his fist.
I'm looking for...
The one who gave me this 'talent'.
Wh-What do we do, sir?
This way. I'll shield you. Leave before the next barrage begins—
O-Out of the way!
Today I'm finally getting out of this hellhole!
Out of the way!
Stay back, sir.
Children and the elderly have priority.
The hell they do—
The Self-Salvation Corps' evacuation route is the safest way. If you insist on leaving on your own...
Be my guest.
...Who are you?
Me? I'm the leader of the Londinium Self-Salvation Corps. You can call me Clovisia.
I heard someone has been helping evacuate the citizens in this district. We appreciate your assistance.
The Self-Salvation Corps will take it from here.
I can still help.
It's dangerous. We can't ask you to risk your life for others.
We cannot promise a reward either.
No, you don't understand.
I'm afraid we don't have time to discuss this...
*sigh*
Long story short, let's just say I've got a sort of curse on me. There's no need to worry about my safety. I'll cover you to help more people evacuate.
Besides, I'm still looking for someone here.
That's the last of them...
Where is that Self-Salvation Corps leader... Clovisia?
Nowell looks around and grabs a man who looks like a worker passing by.
Excuse me—
Ouch!
...Sorry. I didn't notice that your other arm was injured.
I can treat your wound if you like.
Never mind. What do you want?
Have you seen Clovisia, the leader of the Self-Salvation Corps?
She was leading groups of citizens being evacuated. We agreed to meet back here before splitting up, but I haven't seen her.
The citizens have been led to safety. As for her, she's probably still organizing the final evacuation efforts.
I can't be sure, though. We're all busy, after all.
So she was here earlier?
Seems like your routes are quicker and safer than mine.
We know the Londinium underground better, but nothing's completely safe.
Clovisia is needed at the frontline, guiding citizens away from the remaining Sarkaz mercs. You might find her in the lockdown zone, if you're not afraid of the devils' traps.
You seem to have rescued quite a few people yourself. You might be able to help out.
...Thank you.
—Sir! Good sir!
Have you seen my Ollie? My son, with the red-hair. Quiet boy, only about as tall as my knee. Have you seen him?
What?
My son, he's gone... I lost him!
Ollie—
Bloody hell, where did he go?
He kept asking about your grandmother, Colin... Maybe he was worried...
Have you searched the area?
Y-Yes! The folks who came with us helped out too. The only place left is the lockdown zone, but...
That doesn't bother me. I'll go.
But Clovisia is—
Just tell her I've already brought those people out safely.
I'll go with you!
No, you stay here.
No, I mean Clovisia is back!
With a red-haired child in her arms!
—What? Where are they?
Everyone looks in the direction the man is pointing.
That was when everything went wrong...
No...
—Everybody, get back!
It's Ollie! She has Ollie!
Don't go!
Nowell only has time to grab the hysterical woman.
A flash of red as far as the eye can see, and then the ground opens up like a gaping maw, swallowing the leader of the Self-Salvation Corps.
All eyes are trained on the epicenter of the tremor. The one within it holds the child tightly in her arms until the very end.
The dust cloud disperses, and all is quiet again, for now.
Partings like this have become a familiar sight to the people here. The outcome never changes, regardless of the victim.
*cough* *cough*...
Wait! Keep your distance—
Nowell knows all too well that there can sometimes be secondary explosions or aftershocks.
Ollie... Ollie!
Get a search party together! Anyone who can still move!
S-Sir! You're still here! Please, help Ollie—
You're not afraid of the devils' traps, your wounds heal quickly, and you're so brave... Could you at least, b-bring him back...
He's still so little...
In Nowell's long life, there have been precious few things he was able to control. He has an ocean of memories, but it is not up to him which ones float to the surface.
Like an ordinary mortal, he can be distracted by flashbacks of the past that seem to overlap with the present as he gathers the weight of all those years.
This will do.
Are you sure this is where we stop? Are you sure...
...you won't stay?
He's still so little...
My youngest child, he doesn't even know the word 'grandpa' yet...
...Too long.
It's been too long, my child.
I should have gone with your mother, but I've waited so long, long enough for you to grow older than your father.
I've accomplished nothing, spent my life in hesitation, and lingered in this world for far too long. So long, in fact, that I've become a stranger to the children.
If there is one way to end my fate as a bystander...
Now is the time.
Nowell looks towards the scene of the explosion and collapse. In the hole formed from brick and rubble, he seems to see visions of a dark mire from a century past.
It was a morning like this. He had heard the same pleading, which he had, stubbornly, chosen to ignore, instead setting upon a path in search of a death that could never come. This time...
He will not make the same choice again.
...Clovisia shielded the child's head before she fell.
Sir—
There's at least a chance that Ollie survived, but no more than a chance. But that's enough...
I will go and find them.
Guided by the Self-Salvation Corps, Nowell attempts to enter the underground ruins, helped by his years of experience and sheer immortality.
About three hundred meters to the west...
His steps are light and brisk, never stopping, until the sight before his eyes changes.
A red-headed child lies quietly beside a pile of collapsed rubble, near the remnants of an activated Arts Circle.
(Soft breathing)
He hurries forward and takes the child into his arms. The boy's chest rises and falls rhythmically with his breathing.
He could have been asleep in bed, if not for the ash and gravel in his hair.
Phew...
Just passed out, no serious injuries. He was left here by himself, but he didn't fall here directly...
Looks like I made it in time.
Clovisia could be nearby. She put Ollie here—
Clovisia! Are you there?
If you hear me, knock on something hard—
Clovisia?
Can you...
He takes a few steps, and comes face-to-face with the truth.
The young Self-Salvation Corps leader is half-buried in rubble, not stirring in the slightest.
Her eyelids are shut gently, naturally, as though she is only sleeping. Will she sleep here forever, underground?
A moment of pain in a fall that lasted seconds at most, leaving behind only peace. Nowell could not help but think—

lines.st: 'If I could die, would I die with the same expression on my face?'

lines.x: Clovisia!
—Argh!
The aftershock sends gravel flying, like a fowlbeast shaking off water after a rain. It is quick, scattered, and unpredictable.
Nowell did not even have time to check Clovisia for signs of life.
No...

As the place threatens to shake itself apart, he bends over and protects the child from falling rocks with his own body. The sharper pieces easily pierce his clothes and skin, digging deep into flesh and bone.
He remains unmoving, clenching his teeth, waiting for the tremors to cease.
He is awakened from his pained trance by the sound of crying.
The boy had awoken to find himself in a dark, unfamiliar underground space. Following his instincts, he began to cry helplessly.
Good morning, Ollie.
It'll be okay. Everything will be okay. I'm here... it's Nowell.
*cough*, *cough*...
We've met before, remember?
The aftershocks cease. Carrying Ollie with one hand and supporting himself with the other, he forces himself to sit up, pushing the weight off his back.
Fortunately, it is broken fragments of rock rather than a single, heavy slab, but he can still taste blood in his mouth as he moves.
In his arms, the child shakes his head helplessly in the darkness, hardly able to see anything.
...Clovisia.
She saved your life... I'll get you out safely.
Then I'll come back... to bring her back. This is no resting place for her.
Nowell sits down, puts the trembling boy on the ground, and reaches behind to pull the fragments out from his own back.
The boy grabs Nowell's clothes.
Don't worry, I'm not leaving you behind.
(Shakes head)
It's okay. It won't kill me.
Nothing can.
A smile, understood only by him, appears on his lips. Meanwhile, his fingers locate the final fragment, lodged between his scapula.
He grits his teeth and pulls it out.
Argh...
Did I spook you, Ollie? Sorry.
Don't worry. Come with me, and I'll get you out. Whatever it takes.
Enduring pain had become a habit. Nowell lifts the child again and searches his memory for a path that will take them to safety.
I came from... this way.
Hang in there, Ollie. We'll get you out soon. Your parents are waiting—
(Shakes head)
What is it?
(Struggles)
Uh—
You want to get down? It's quicker and safer if I carry you. Don't you want to see your mum sooner?
(Jumps down)
...Alright. Stick close to me, and don't let go.
The child nods and grabs the corner of Nowell's clothes. He even wraps it around his palm tightly without being told, indicating that he understands Nowell.
Alright. Let's—
...You have walked a long path...
...Poor soul...
Suddenly, he hears an ethereal voice echoing in every direction.
A mist spreads in the darkness, surrounding him.
Why have you come?
Nowell can recognize that voice anywhere.
He cannot move. A sudden, cold fury buffets his reason, nailing him to the spot.
What do you want?
Stop.
He does not know why the voice chose this moment to appear. Instinctive mistrust overcomes all judgment.
Stop your parlor tricks.
I thought you wanted to meet me.
What are you talking about?!
Who are you? Where are you?
I am here. You came for me, and I came for you.
All you need to do... is open your eyes.

Nowell has never seen the owner of the voice, nor gathered any clues about their appearance. Over the long years, all he could do was to piece together rumor after rumor, letting questions simmer into hatred.
If this voice is real and not his imagination, then it must...
Is that so? Then I'm sure you can tell me...
Are you the wish-granting fairy? Or the powerful 'Nation's Sentinel'?
The unicorn, hidden in the shadows of history, is both benefactor and villain.
I am all that, and none of that.
I am merely here now to promise reparation to you, poor soul.
You don't even know my name. What I went through must have been insignificant, hardly a footnote to a legend like you, compared to the affairs you've meddled with.
That is untrue.
You harbored a possibility that I placed hope in. How would one little weed of unknown breed grow if it never wilts? And how will it affect the lands around it?
That was my thought when I bestowed immortality upon you. What should have been a blessing.
Blessing. Pft.
Your arrogant 'blessing' has brought me nothing but an eternity of pain.
...That was not my intention.
But that's what it became. Death by a thousand cuts, except you wouldn't even let me die!
It's nothing but a curse!
I understand your anger.
Tell me. What will make you feel better?
...Hah.
What, will you 'bestow' another 'blessing' upon me?
Nowell can feel the child grab his clothes even tighter.
Can you hear me, Ollie?
(Nods)
This voice is a liar. Don't listen to it. Don't think about it.
I'll take you out of here.
Whoever you are, you can ask those legendary heroes who dance with you. Me, I'm leaving.
Few are those who hold the helm in the tides of history.
Are you complaining? To me?
...If you consider my blessing a curse, I might offer you reparations.
Make a wish. What is it that you want?

lines.st: A name, thinks Nowell. Just a name. This bastard, this villain who stands ever on high because of her powers, has long forgotten the weed's name.

lines.x: You have a chance... You have already gained much more than the common man.

lines.st: She must be filled with pity as she gazes upon me—
'You poor, insignificant creature. I will help you. I will help you, always.'
'Let us wipe away the tragic past with a beautiful future.'

lines.x: Haha.
Have you made up your mind?
Let's go, Ollie. We've wasted enough time here.
(Grabs Nowell's clothes tightly)
Yes, child, I'm here.
Don't be afraid of the voice. It's just a bad guy, no different from the ones ravaging the city above.
I'll come back to catch it later. But first, I need to get you out.
Phew...
—It's Ollie! Mr. Nowell found him!
Over here! A hand!
Careful, child.
I'll lift you up. Take it easy—
No worries, sir! I'm up here! I'll grab him!
Your turn, Mr. Nowell! Are you hurt? Do you need a hand?
No need.

lines.st: Nowell clambers up friskily, but stops at the edge of the tunnel entrance rather than walking towards the crowd gathered not too far away.
He looks up again at the sun shrouded by thick clouds. It is one of countless sunrises he has seen, but this one is a little different, because of those who have lived to see it.

lines.x: Ollie! My baby!
Oh, the fright you gave me! Next time, I'm holding onto you and never letting go... I won't let this happen again!
Wh-Where did this blood come from? Are you hurt?
(Shakes head)
Strange, I don't see any wounds...
(Looks at Nowell)
Ollie, where's the lady who found you first? Is she still...
(Shakes head)
She wasn't down there?
(Shakes head)
Oh, you're always like this... Well, it's all good. I'm so glad you came back safely.
Thank you so much, Mr. Nowell—
The man waves at Nowell with something other than anxiety or panic on his face.
It is a smile of gratitude, with a sincerity that makes Nowell awkward. All he can do is nod and smile back.
You're... welcome...

But the man keeps walking until he is right in front of him, and reaches out a hand, as though words alone cannot express his gratitude. And so Nowell keeps the smile on his face and shakes the extended hand, almost unconsciously.
Only then does he finally realize that pain is spreading, from his back to his chest to the rest of his body. He has difficulty breathing and bends down, grabbing his own collar.
The wounds he received from the rubble have still not started to heal.
—Sir? Are you okay?!
My goodness, your back! I didn't realize you were so badly injured—we have to find a doctor!
Oh, how silly of me, you're a doctor yourself...
I'm... injured?
Th-The wound on your back! It's still bleeding! It's so big... and there's more than one!
Are they not healing yet?
N-No. That's normal for us, but I suppose it must be strange to you.
What happened down there? How did you get hurt?
That leader of the Self-Salvation Corps is down there too, isn't she?
She protected Ollie. I have to do something for her... At least bring her body back.
Clovisia.
Her name was Clovisia? That does ring a bell now that you mention it. I didn't even get a good look at her before it all happened.
I heard she was a great woman.

lines.st: I'm the leader of the Londinium Self-Salvation Corps. You can call me Clovisia.
'I am all that, and none of that.'
We cannot promise a reward either.
'I am merely here now to promise reparation to you, poor soul.'
I'm afraid we don't have time to discuss this...
'Tell me. What will make you feel better?'

lines.x: A great woman?
Hah...
I don't...
I don't know.
Clovisia... he had passed right by the one he spent his entire life looking for, without even realizing it.
But it is no longer important. With great effort, Nowell looks up, fighting against the pain and difficulty of breathing.
In his blurry vision, he sees a child clutching his mother's hand tightly. The boy is the very picture of health, his hair and skin a warm color, with a smile on his face, just like...
I-I'll take you to the Self-Salvation Corps! Or the Exemplars! They must have medics!
No...
What? Why?

lines.st: Just like the sun.

lines.x: M-Mr. Nowell! Are you okay?
Hang in there!
Nowell falls to his knees, no longer able to support his own weight. It is as though the fatigue of a hundred years of wandering has come upon him in an instant.
Before his hands and feet grow cold from blood loss, he curls his body down, ignoring the searing pain as the wounds on his back stretch, until his head touches the ground.
It is a posture of worship and reverence, but he is not praying to anyone.
He lifts his head after a little while.
The leader of the Self-Salvation Corps was not in the ruins.
The pain lingers, as does the urge to cry his heart out. But all is silent, and only the beating of the heart resonates in his chest.
The thing that would make him happy, the thing that can kill him... it is already here.
What was that?
Clovisia wasn't there.
What about your injuries—
Right now, rather than spending my time on futile efforts...
I should spend it on saving more people.

lines.st: The soil of this foreign land is soaked with the blood of the Sarkaz.
Churned, jumbled. I can sense a chaotic mess of emotions tangled together.
Hesitation, wrath, cowardice, homesickness. Was this what you wished for, Your Highness?
Sleep, my kin. As long as blood continues to flow, relief from pain will soon follow.
...Have I arrived too late? Perhaps.
If the war can truly come to an end, if this end can truly determine who wins and who loses... When have we Sarkaz ever won, I ask?
I have never been fond of watching the clock, mind you. I prefer to keep time with the pulsing of my heart.
It told me to come here, and so I came.

lines.x: As far as I was aware, Rose Riverside was dissolved after your princess fled far away and the Canoes scattered.
Quite a few former Canoes have gone on to serve the Military Commission.
Reestablishing our long-lost contact right after the Commission's decline suggests you want something from me. I can't guarantee you'll get it.
Not even for old-time's sake? You're the same as ever, Kal'tsit.
It's hard to believe how much Amiya has grown. No doubt she's keeping an eye on me, yes?
A necessary precaution.
Logos warned us of your appearance.
Since we withdrew from the falling airship, you are the first outsider to reach our safe house.
...'Outsider'.
Are you like me, Dame Kal'tsit—most difficult to persuade when we're in the throes of grief?
This is not a Babel safe house, Entelechia.
Hearing that codename coming from your lips truly takes me back.
I passed by the charnel house that is Londinium on my way here. I rescued a few fortunate souls, and sent off a few unfortunate ones.
It brings me no joy to see my countrymen spill their blood on foreign soil, Kal'tsit.
I don't recall you being such a sentimental person.
The Vampire goes silent. Kal'tsit sees she has no intention of answering.
Why would you risk crossing the battlefields of Victoria to see me, Entelechia?
If you require assistance, Theresis would be the better option.
No need for these probing questions, Kal'tsit. I'd rather not have anything to do with the Regent. Perhaps some of the sisters agree with him, but as you know...
Ever since Her Highness Theresa's untimely accident at Babel, there has been less and less contact between us. Our sororal bonds have weakened.
But that is not why I came to find you, Kal'tsit. I intend to help Ascalon with a trifling matter, one that will allow her to enter the city a tad easier.
What do you want?
According to our latest intelligence, the Military Commission is strengthening its control within the city.
The Nachzehrer troops and the ducal armies are facing each other down at the city walls. The situation is ever-changing.
Oh, I know, I know. But such an adorable and studious youngster as her is well worth the effort to look after.
That is not what I mean. Given the circumstances, you should have been unable to find any trace of Ascalon. I am very familiar with her ways.
She is wounded. Gravely so.
She's never used something like that as an excuse to lower her guard.
You really trust her. It's hard to say whether I should be happy or worried for her that you hold her in such high regard.
Who is helping you with this?
Trickster, one of our sharp-eyed sisters. As a technician, she was able to hunt down a few clues I was looking for in Londinium.
Her? I remember you all cherished her and would never have approved of putting her in the middle of this battle. And according to the vows made by all you Canoes, none of you should even be here.
...As I suspected, you know of our plight. Her intelligence was right. You have a trail leading to the princess.
As I'm sure you're well aware, Dame, I am the only one who will not and can not leave this river of blood.
Do you still doubt me, even after all I said?
Rhodes Island thanks you for your assistance. We will repay the Canoes in kind, just as Theresa did during the Babel years.
I will tell you all I know in regard to that princess.
Thank you, Kal'tsit.
On another note, while I was in the bowels of the city, I caught a whiff of Duq'arael's stench. While I would love to help settle that matter for you while I'm there—
I will need help from your people.
I'm afraid Rhodes Island does not have any more to spare inside the city.
There is a local faction known as Millscar. Finding them should not be a challenge for you.
They are close to the heart of the storm, but are determined to avoid entangling themselves with any of the involved parties. A stance you would find familiar, I'm sure.
You would trust someone like that?
We retain shared interests, for the time being.
If the Military Commission has other plans for Duq'arael's arrangements, they will undoubtedly affect the ducal alliance's next moves... or even disrupt the Exemplars' plans to enter the city.
Neither Rhodes Island nor Millscar wishes to see such an outcome.
Haha. You really haven't changed one bit. Did this plan come from you, or that commander of yours?
It is Rhodes Island's plan.
...Rhodes Island. Very well, do send my regards to your commander. Though I fear I'm simply another lost memory at this point.
Dame, there is a question that's been eating away at me, one I was hoping you could answer.
Aside from His Highness the Regent, I'm afraid only you hold the answer. After all, you were the only ones who made it from the airship.
Is a question you already know the answer to really so important?
...Of course it is.
If one does not see it with one's own eyes, hear it with one's own ears, say it with one's own mouth, then saying goodbye becomes quite a challenge.
Even though my heart sensed the echo of the myriad souls as they left with such clarity, I still ask myself over and over if she was among them.
And so I must ask... Has Her Highness left us once more?

lines.loc: Southern Region of KazdelSummer, 1094, after the fall of Babel

lines.x: *cough*, *cough*...
I've left you your life, and a knife. I'd say that's a very generous price for answering my question.
Who hired you all to kill me? Few even know of our existence.
I'm dead either way, so do me a favor and be quick about it—
'Release' him.
I mean with a quick death like he asked for, Entelechia.
As long as he spills enough blood, you can always just tear out the memories you want from him, right?
Well, since you mention it—
The Vampire raises her hand.
The crimson shackles binding the mercenary disappear into nothingness.
I find I'm rather against that suggestion.
Go on then, mercenary. As you can see, I have far too many people trying to kill me at the moment. I shan't be remembering your face.
No need to thank me.
Watching that cringeworthy performance of yours was painful.
Not many know the name 'Rose Riverside'. I didn't have to do anything to him to guess who's hunting us.
Her Highness has only just left us, and those who have hidden in the shadows for too long are only too eager to act.
You played your part well, though. Otherwise, it would have been such a bother finding an excuse to let him go.
Don't misunderstand me. Her Highness is dead, and I have no intention of following the Regent to throw my life away in Victoria.
Which is why I found my own way forward, one that lets me live.
It almost sounds like you want to kill me too, Milton.
Even if I did, I couldn't touch you... or any of you, for that matter.
If not, I'd have taken back my sister a long time ago.
You best make yourself scarce, then.
The young mixed-blood Sarkaz does not do so, instead tightening his grip on his knife.
Hah.
You helped your sister deliver messages, which is why I remember you. My memories hold true, and whatever your role may be now, it has no bearing on them—
You and I both know what Her Highness's passing means for Kazdel.
I sense a storm approaching, and none of us can escape it.
I do not wish for your sister to mourn. Leave while you still can.
The situation is rather murky at the moment, and the sisters have scattered. Even I am having trouble tracking them down.
The chaos in Kazdel has forced me to bid many a farewell. There are fewer and fewer informants I can speak to as the days go by.
I do not intend on losing another sister because of you.
...There's nowhere any of you can hide.
Most of the people after you aren't doing it out of hatred, but for the information you have.
Even if the Military Commission says they won't hunt down anyone with ties to Babel, do you really think they'd let you waltz away with all those secrets spilling out of your pockets?
Hmph.
I'd like to see them try and stop me.
You're on a fool's errand, Milton.
I'm just a worthless merc, but if I really do manage to bring your head in, at least I'll have a chance to protect my sister.
I'm out of options, Entelechia!
With a burst of speed, the young Sarkaz swings his sword, but Entelechia's scythe does not block his attack as he expected.
Instead, the Vampire's body slowly dissipates like a fog of blood, fading into the background.
Tch.
You could have been more honest from the start.
If I promise you that none living shall approach this street, will you speak with candor?
Yes, exactly what you're thinking. Your friends in ambush will not be coming.
You're not as calm as you're pretending to be either.
You pretend your pursuers bother you and claim you acted only to expose a traitor.
Then you send everyone who finds you back to reveal your location, all so that anyone with a grudge against the Canoes will focus solely on you.
I heard from my sister that you were the one who cleaned up after every operation. The cleaner, they call you.
Once when she barely escaped by the skin of her teeth, you used a similar method to draw attention to yourself.
Though it involved faking an injury that time. You were losing a lot of blood, true, but the Arts you used was powerful enough to turn everyone who even glanced at you into a fine red mist.
My sister risked her life to let you all 'cleanse' her blood, turning her into a pure Vampire... but even she couldn't eat for days after seeing that.
I did endeavor not to splash too much blood. I'm not fond of working in such a garish fashion myself—
No. What I hate is your arrogance.
You think you can bear the entire blood debt of Rose Riverside by yourself.
...And what makes you say that?
If I really were as capable as you claim, I would have held on to my sisters and have them accompany me upon this river of blood.
And Rose Riverside would not have scattered so prematurely...
I wish I'd had time to do more than clean-up duty during the crux of the civil war. I was so busy I didn't even hear the whispers in the wind.
And that was that. I could not save Theresa, nor a single Canoe.
Even what you're willing to do for your sister now, I cannot do myself.
What the—?!
Shh.
A sparkling, blood-red gem slowly falls in front of Milton, as Entelechia's countenance grows even paler.
Take it back to the one you believe can save your sister. There are only two people in this entire land who can tell the difference between it and my Little Garnet. I can promise you that much.
Your... earring?
More than that... but you needn't know. Now go.
I wish you the best of luck, just as I do for my sisters.
What about you?
Judging by what you're holding, am I not already slain by your hand?
If you have the chance to see your sister again, tell her I will find that woman, and have her provide an answer to all us sisters.
This princess of yours, is she a traitor? A liar? Or something else?
By the time the scent of blood wafts over in the breeze, she has already gone.
...Thank you.
Can you hear me?
I'm inside the Military Commission's command center. The communications shutdown has been lifted.
That's mighty quick of you, Entelechia.
Naturally. Same old trick of mine.
While I was dealing with Duq'arael's altar, I found quite a few people lying in pools of blood. Some were closer to the Military Commission than others.
There was still information on the command center in their memories.
Ah... Seems my divination today was off the mark.
Today's blood drops were in the shape of two swords. Thought it meant you would have to face off against the Royal Court's army and have a tough time getting in.
Still playing at fortune teller, are you?
The sound of something falling passes through the wireless communicator, distant and fuzzy.
...Are you alright over there?
Dammit, tripped over a cable again.
Anyway, with a body as bad as mine, I'm not casting any crazy blood Arts any time soon. Just let me mess around with a couple of drops now and then, okay?
Won't you consider telling me where you're hiding right now?
Why fix what isn't broken? Even back in Rose Riverside, when we Canoes were close as anything, nobody knew my hiding place.
But now that you're in Londinium, there's nowhere that can guarantee your safety.
No need to worry about me.
Found the target, to the northeast. Sounds like there are two teams. Make sure to avoid them.
Understood.
We may have destroyed the Commission's command structure, but the mercenaries who are taking it over are unfamiliar faces.
—Bzzzzz...
...Are you still there?
I'm here. Sisters working together, just like old times.
Yes, just like old times.
The three in charge of those mercenaries had some past dealings with Babel.
I may be here at Kal'tsit's request, but I doubt we would get very far using our old friendship as leverage.
Would you say this goes against our sisterly vows?
I'd say it all depends on how you see it. No Canoe shall interfere in matters related to Babel... It was you who set that rule.
Some people don't really mind killing their own, you know.
With more rules comes less trouble. I know what us sisters are like... and how bad the blood can run between us at times.
Yep. Some join, so obviously there'll be some who leave too.
We have to accept that, Entelechia.
This does not explain her sudden disappearance, though. I should think that I know her the best.
Obviously. No one else has got a single drop of her blood on them.
Which means her departure was truly without warning, rather than us missing a sign.
Sounds like you've doubted yourself.
Join the club. It was only after it happened that I really got into this 'divination' thing.
It's never accurate, more like a game of associations that gets you thinking. But at least it gives me some sort of warning, reminding me to be mentally ready for any and all changes.
I'll help you out. At the very least, we know she came by Londinium.
Two heads are better than one, and one clue's better than none, right—
Hold on. There's movement outside the command center. Victorian alliance troops are locking horns with a group of unknown Sarkaz.
Those would be the mercenaries who have come to take charge.
It would seem they're right behind us.
I'll tidy up this command center for them then, save them the trouble later.
Would you mind leading the way?
No problem. I'm always here.

lines.loc: LondiniumAfter Babel's seizure of the Military Commission command center

lines.x: Entelechia.
...You're late, Kal'tsit.
Drafting a final plan of attack demanded my time. It's rare to see you absent-minded.
Rhodes Island's medical operators can provide standard medical aid, but if a Vampire does not wish to staunch their own bleeding, I'm afraid there is little they can do.
It's nothing. Who doesn't bleed a little in battle?
I was simply pondering the shape this bloodstain has taken. It seems like a bad omen, no?
But do go on, Kal'tsit.
...Forgive me, but as usual, I can only thank you in a personal capacity for destroying Duq'arael's remaining Arts circles and saving Londinium from yet another calamity.
To be fair, I only did so out of my loathing for Duq'arael.
It wasn't to help you, and certainly not to help those Victorians.
Nonetheless, your action helped clear many obstacles for Wiš'adel and her people.
True, true. I trust you'll remember my help today, Dame.
Naturally. About the information you gleaned from within the Arts circles of blood, have you told Millscar?
Just a hint, but that little kitten is smart enough to figure it out.
When the Victorians and Sarkaz began hostilities, it was inevitable that some among them would seek cooperation over common interests.
This war must end. These problems must be left to Londinium, to Victoria itself.
Amiya and the Doctor are currently fighting their own fight with the Victorian delegation to bring things to a peaceful close.
If negotiations go as we hope, all the Sarkaz will leave at the appointed time. The tragedy of Londinium can end.
The more one knows about the past, the more worries it add to their brow.
It almost sounds like you're mocking yourself, O Nearly-All-Knowing Dame Kal'tsit.
Four years ago, I found a Babel in complete disarray. We may have had our similarities in that sense.
Us cleaners really hate having to leave, and are disinclined to accept defeat. So I stayed, wanting to piece back together all the shattered fragments.
...As promised, I've synced what I know about your princess to the frequency band you provided. It's just scraps of information, but it should be enough to help you.
