Thank you, Kal'tsit.
If you and Rhodes Island still need any assistance, do tell me now. Getting all the Sarkaz to agree to leave is hardly the easiest matter, after all.
There is one thing you must know. The person receiving this information did not respond.
Closure managed to triangulate the approximate location of the band signal, albeit with great difficulty, but our people were prevented from approaching.
No, rather than 'prevented', they were being distracted. This sort of activity can only mean there are people in the shadows with their own purposes.
After me, perhaps?

lines.st: 'No need to be in such a rush, Entelechia. Run as fast as you like, but that bouquet will wilt before you get past that battlefield no matter what.'
'There are no roses in Kazdel, true. Even if you do bring some for your sisters to see, they might not like them. You should toss the bouquet once the flowers are wilted.'
'I am also unsure what awaits us once we return to the city of the Sarkaz.'
'Though with how well you've become in combat and Arts, there should only be one thing you need to be ready for.'
'I hope you have learned how to grieve, how to say farewell, Entelechia. It is easy to live with a heart full of hate, but it is hard to bear the pain of love.'
'Saying farewell is what causes us to grieve. And so, I will see Her Highness Theresa's wish fulfilled.'
'I want you there with me when it becomes fulfilled.'
'Will you agree to that, Entelechia?'

lines.x: Entelechia enters the ruins.
A few frightened civilians follow her at a distance, their eyes locked onto her, muttering curses under their breath. None dare to throw the bricks they hold in their hands.
A dried streak of blood trails across the ground, as if someone was dragged. It winds past a fallen wall, wrecked in the fighting, and skirts around a rusty assembly line.
Trickster lies motionless, her face drained of blood, a shattered receiver still clutched tightly in her arms.
Blood drips out of the terrifying wound on her, crimson as ever.
A frail Vampire died in Londinium, a sight all too common. Nobody cares why she died, but nobody can take away her dignity in death.
Your blood still isn't dry... Someone has kept the last of your dignity.
...Only someone living can leave behind minute Arts such as this, Arts of life—
My Princess, is this... is this all the mercy you could spare?

lines.st: 'You ended up coming here after all, Entelechia.'

lines.x: As her finger dips into the blood, a host of information springs up before her eyes. Through this blood, Entelechia communes with the consciousness of the deceased before it disperses completely.
...Who killed you?

lines.st: 'I hid away too long and got hungry, decided to go out for a walk. Someone hit me. I knew I couldn't escape, so I didn't bother to stop the bleeding.'

lines.x: As Rose Riverside's 'cleaner', she has seen too many of her compatriots fallen in pools of their own blood.
The shadows of these grisly scenes serve as elucidators of information in her mind's eye, fitting together into a vague memory.
Was it the Victorians, or the Military Commission—

lines.st: 'No need to be mad at anyone about this. If Rose Riverside hadn't taken me in, a Vampire as weak as me wouldn't have made it past the civil war.'
'You all always said I was the only one of us sisters with sharp eyes, so whenever any surprises happened, even a small breakdown at the Furnace, I'd be the first you all fussed over.'
'That's all I ever needed. I'm so glad I met you all.'

lines.x: The memories continue further into the past. A familiar figure steps out of the river of blood, quietly watching them.
It is Trickster's memory, and the memories of all the Canoes. As the warmth dies away in the blood, so too does the memory of the dead slowly dry up.
Not many Canoes have left them, but none left in silence. No matter how dire the situation, they always spared the time to grieve for a lost sister.
At least, they did, once upon a time.
No, wait—What about the people we're looking for? Are you really alright with this?
Didn't you want to bring back all our sisters too?
She sees no information. There is no answer from the blood.
But unexpectedly, her finger touches something solid.

lines.st: 'Take away your hand. Do not tarry in this river of blood.'

lines.x: She knows this crystal. Of course she does.
It is a drop of her own blood. A vow. The beginning of Rose Riverside.
There is no information within the pure crystal, merely the lingering warmth of Arts.
My Princess... Why did you choose to return this to me now? Why now, of all times?
You were here, so why did you leave so soon?
Was it because you could not save her? Or was it because of the war that barely ended? Because of all that transpired years ago?
Or maybe... it's simply to say farewell...
Not planning on staying in Londinium?
I have found the answer I sought.
That princess of yours did come by this city.
Neither the Military Commission's information network nor our covert web in Londinium could find anything on her. This is not good for us.
She did not come here for the war.
'Rose Riverside will no longer take part in anything to do with Babel.' A rule I set myself. She would not have violated it.
Kal'tsit, she may have simply come to say farewell to Her Highness... Or perhaps it was to stop Trickster and I from continuing our search...
The sudden disappearance of your princess on the eve of Babel's collapse is suspicious enough. I cannot even confirm if she left on Theresa's orders.
Until we know where she truly stands, Rhodes Island cannot rule out the possibility that she might interfere with our plans.
Even if I too wish to believe that she has simply come to say her goodbyes.
I will find her, Kal'tsit. The final clues Trickster left for me will be enough.
She owes me an answer, too.
If you need assistance, we are still happy to—
I know you are going to say that Rhodes Island wants to help.
But Rhodes Island and Babel are very different beasts. I do not wish for Rhodes Island to be ensnared in the past once again. I believe Her Highness would not want it either.
Our name may have changed, Entelechia, but our mission hasn't.
Rhodes Island will not continue down Babel's doomed path, but we will never deny the ideals Babel showed us.
Besides, I want the truth too.
...I thank you for your kindness.
A drifting canoe needs its berth, and for us, that can only be Kazdel...
The Royal Court, shaped by Duq'arael, reeks with a stench that displeases the sisters. No doubt, sooner or later, another vile creature will emerge from his pool of blood.
Perhaps I will rope in some other Canoes in due time, to cross the river of blood once more, and return to the city Her Highness built.
I foresee Kazdel having even greater need of us in the wake of this war than Babel ever did.
I fear persuading those sisters of yours will not be easy. Not to mention your drifter of a princess.
I believe in the vows of us Canoes, even if many of us detest each other.
Send this petal of Trickster's to Kazdel in my stead, Dame. See her home.
She slowly opens her hand, revealing a sparkling, blood-red crystal. She did all she could to preserve the longing lingering in her sister's veins.
You have my promise, and Rhodes Island's as well.
Then don't blame me when trouble comes knocking on your door.
It has been a long time since Rhodes Island was as weak as you imagine, Entelechia.
We can only hope. Goodbye, Kal'tsit.

lines.st: 'The warmth in this drop of blood will never die, just as us Canoes will never drown in the river of blood. This is our vow.'
'I promise you...'
'For our farewell, I shall grieve. I would much prefer to part less often, but...'
'...Goodbye, Entelechia.'
Can you hear Victoria cry, o kings of yore?
Fires burn my mother's skin...
I fly above her head, but do not hear her cry for help.
Only lament.
But who does she lament for—

lines.x: Don't bother collecting the bodies of the Steam Knights and mercenaries. The Victorians already built this tomb for them.
I know, Ines, but...
Paprika clenches the blood-stained piece of paper in her hand and tries to imagine what it felt like as the Steam Knight wrote these words.
This is the Steam Knights' home, but the Sarkaz shouldn't be left here.
If Hoederer can really bring everyone back to Kazdel with the Lifebone, can we take them with us too?
If you've already made up your mind, then go ahead.
But are you sure about this, Paprika? You aren't from Kazdel.
I—I can always find a way back to Columbia if I need to—
It's safer to travel back with the group for now.

Who knows what they'd do to you if you stayed in Victoria, or tried to head back to Columbia now?
Given the circumstances—
Ngh!
Stop pretending you're not injured.
I won't die. I can't die. Besides, we're already taking too long.
How is the Lifebone's recovery going?
It'll make it to Kazdel... provided no one 'accidentally' damages it again.
Oh, was that meant for me?
Guess YOUR recovery's going just fine.
How are things looking outside, W?
I'll let you off the hook about the name this time, Ines.
Even better. How are things looking outside, W?
The Victorians made a big push right after the fire on the walls. The Nachzehrer lines collapsed right away.
What about the Nachzehrer King?
Still kicking, but he retreated into the city—
I—Is there any news about the General, Miss Wiš'adel?
Nope. Could be dead for all I know.
Manfred wouldn't die so easily.
But the chaos outside is a problem.
Based on intel my guys collected, Nezzsalem is still sitting cozy, even though the Military Commission screwed this battle up big time.
And while I'd love to blow Theresis to smithereens, I gotta admit...
He's a cunning enough bastard that there's no way his forces would back down that easy.
I'm thinking your plan needs to be moved up, Hoederer.
The Lifebone is ready.
Whatever the Regent is scheming, we should prepare to leave with the ones who've been dragged into this mess... before the Sarkaz purge begins.
I doubt the Victorians would let them go so easily.
I know, but...
Since when did we need the Victorians' permission to do anything?
I've got an idea.
But first, we'll need to capture the Commission's command center. Say, in the name of Babel.
You guys just wait here for my orders—
An idea isn't a plan, W, but you seem confident.
Fine, let's go. I've spent enough time underground.
Er, doesn't Hoederer need someone to take care of him?
I have it on good authority that he won't die.
...Yes, that's what I said.
Whatever the Regent and Nachzehrer are planning at the Palace of Westhaleg, the fate of the survivors is probably not their priority.
We can't just sit back and watch. You can tell us about your idea on the way.
Hmph. Two dunces.
What about you, Yarnball?
I'll go with you! The General might be at the command center. I have so much to ask him!
But do we just leave the Lifebone here?
I could blow it to pieces if you want. If you're volunteering to carry everyone on your back instead.
It's WIŠ'ADEL!
Don't worry, Yarnball, no one'll ever guess it's here. And if anyone dares get close, my guys will take care of them.
Wiš'adel surveys the resting place of kings, now barely-recognizable rubble.
Besides, what better place to hide a pile of bones than a graveyard?

lines.loc: Outside the Military Commission Command Center

lines.x: Found a way in yet?
The ducal alliance's army is surrounding the place, but they don't seem to have any intention of attacking.
What are they doing, then? Out for a picnic?
Defending this place was so important to the General. How could...
The General? You mean that devil by the name of Manfred? I'm afraid he fled with his tail between his legs, abandoning all of you.
Her Grace was right. The Military Commission's spies do come through here.
Who the hell are you calling Commission spies?
We are not your enemies. We've come to end this war.
I've read your file, Sarkaz. 'The Right-Hand of Manfred'. Your word can hardly be taken at face value.
Then maybe you've also read how I escaped from Manfred.
I'm afraid I lacked the patience to read through the files of every insignificant devil.
We've surrounded the place, sir.
Eliminate these Sarkaz spies.
Search their bodies for intel on the command center. I've had enough of the Sarkaz's tricks.
Hah! So you're not attacking 'cause you're too scared of what's inside?!
Well, if you don't have the guts, we'll do it for you.
Are you sure you want to pick a fight now, W? I can ensure a safe retreat, at least.
(Pulls weapon) I'm with her. The Sarkaz cannot afford to wait.
Protect Paprika, Ines. We're forcing our way through.
If you say so.
Shadows spread from Ines's feet, locking on to every Victorian soldier present.
Then, she senses a different kind of shadow.
...Delphine?
Let them through, sir.
And here I was thinking you finally made up your mind to kill us.
I am not under the command of the Exemplars, my lady. Nor do I want to be put on 'mandatory leave' like Bellingham. I am simply executing the Duke's orders—
'Wipe out the Sarkaz.' No more, no less.
These Sarkaz share our strategic objective.
They aren't a monolith.
You're here to kill the Nachzehrer, aren't you, Hoederer?
The Nachzehrer King holds great significance to the Sarkaz, but he cannot be allowed to live.
Only the swift and complete defeat of his forces will convince the ones sitting on the fence to willingly withdraw with us.
...You want to flee with the devils?
Got a problem with that, punk?
You do understand the crimes they committed, do you not?
...I can't deny them.
But I also understand what will happen if large numbers of leaderless Sarkaz mercenaries are left scattered across the countryside.
Mercenaries who've tasted blood in war.
You want to protect them.
And prevent further chaos.
Can you give your guarantee?
No. But she can. Babel can.
Hoederer points towards Wiš'adel.
This war has long since lost its last justification for taking lives. It's time to bring it to an end. This has nothing to do with who I was, or who I am now.
Very well. You can go in.
If Rhodes Island trusts you, then—
You should be careful, Lady Delphine. What you are doing may constitute treason.
I don't need your advice.
Lieutenant Shearer, instruct the Exemplars units to escort this Sarkaz team into the command center.
Inform all alliance units that this order comes directly from Delphine Windermere.
Understood, Lady Windermere.
I hope you will be able to answer for yourself when the Dukes hear about this.
I have even more reason to hate the Sarkaz than most of you, but I don't let it blind myself to the current situation.
Londinium has bled enough.
Thank you for your help.
It's not you I'm helping, Sarkaz. Go end this war.
As for you, sir, you can either continue this staring match with me, or we can both go back with our men and prepare for the next battle.
Or do you need to call the Duke of Caster for instructions?
No need for that, Lady Delphine. Her Grace has granted me discretion. I agree that this is not our battlefield.
I will, however, report all the facts pertinent to this incident to Her Grace.
Suit yourself.
While you're at it, send her my regards, and a promise from the Exemplars—
'When the horn of the final battle sounds, the Exemplars will be the first to answer.'
I get that things are messed up at the Commission, but is this really all they have guarding the command center?
According to Dame Kal'tsit and the Doctor, Rose Riverside has probably paved the way for us.
What about Manfred and his men? Afraid of getting blown up or something?
If the General is still... alive, there's only one place he would be—by the Regent's side.
We'll have to count on the Doctor's group being able to handle the Regent.
It's all yours, Wiš'adel.
And I thought I'd have to fight you for the mic.
Your first broadcast more than did the job. 'Wiš'adel'...
Why so serious all of a sudden? About to spew a bunch of crap I don't understand again?
(Shakes head) The name Her Highness gave you holds many meanings.
I know.
If they need to hear a new voice, they'll hear it. Before that, though, I want to set things up.
We still need the command center to organize the withdrawal. Don't blow it up in a fit of anger.
Blow it up? Of course I wanna blow it up. But there's only one of it, which means I can't try all the different ways I had planned for it.
Don't rush me, Ines. This time, I'm gonna blow it into the sky, so everyone can see it.
See who we are, and what we want to do.
Listen up, people! Babel's captured the Military Commission's command center.
Those bastards don't know their heads from their asses right now, and nobody's got it in them to help out the crusty old Nachzehrer King.
So quit wasting time and get those feet moving!
Believe me or not, whatever, just step on the gas and get this over with!
Oh, and just saying, don't forget to keep your eyes on the sky.
My Patriarch, that...
The command structure of the Commission has collapsed. Temporary chaos is inevitable... but no matter.
The long oppression may have numbed the Sarkaz, but this war has reignited their hatred.
A moment of fear or hesitation will not extinguish it.
The Nachzehrer King looks up to see a frenzied flame cut open the veil of decay for an instant.
Besides, there will be another to stand up and lead them.
'Babel'...

lines.st: The first time I saw the Palace of Westhaleg was fifteen years ago, on a fifty-pound bill.
It was the first time I held a wrench in my hands, and the first time I held money I'd earned myself.
I rubbed the bill with my numb and tired hands. The factory door began to close behind me, but I felt as though the door to all of Terra was being opened before my eyes.
A place that seemed so far away, a place where kings once sat, was right there in my hands.

lines.x: Why'd we stop?
The area ahead is completely shrouded in fog. Captain Horn's orders are to halt the advance until we figure what tricks the Nachzehrer are up to in there.
Look at all the pretty patterns on the tiles beneath our feet, though. We're close to the palace.
Be patient, and grab a proper weapon from camp. The Sarkaz protecting this place are the worst of the worst. A wrench isn't gonna cut it.
This wrench has smashed five Sarkaz skulls just on the way here!
I can do it, whether you believe it or not.
It's been fifteen years since I felt like this, but now... I feel like I can take that palace, Nachzehrer King be damned.
Wait... shush.
What is it?
Fog. I'll report it in. Find a safe place to hide.
You hear that sound from inside the fog?
All I hear is the wind. Same sound as when I forgot to shut the gym's windows at night.
If it's the wind, it's blowing against something! A flag, or clothes!
Tell Vina—
In an instant, innumerable tattered rags rush out of the fog, drifting across the sky above the city like a gray-white tide.
There is cloth freshly torn from clothing, and there are banners woven by hatred over the millennia.
They have wrapped generation after generation of Nachzehrer warriors, hiding the rot beneath, but now they fly freely in the sky, inviting all they touch to walk into decay.
Bloody hell, I can't free my leg!
I got you!
Get away from the cloth! I'll cover you!
They'll corrode any weapons they touch! Melee units, pull back and let the casters deal with them!
1st and 3rd Caster Battalions! Volley on my mark!
I can't get rid of them!
The palls are immersed in decay, the shroud drawing in death. Even the formless witchcraft of the Nachzehrer has rules that can be followed.
But I am unable to decipher this fog...
It is neither Arts, nor natural.
Is this some new tactic, Logos?

opts: They wouldn't wait this long to try a proven tactic.

lines.x: Perhaps Nezzsalem is changing... or perhaps it's the Nachzehrer themselves that are changing.
This place will soon be more dangerous than the shroud we stepped into on the battlefield outside the city.
The other units must have encountered the same interference. Even sending out a signal is difficult.
This three-hundred meter plaza is enough to delay a thousand men for days.

opts: We must find Theresis.
Theresis still has Amnannam.
Analyze the shroud, Logos.
How's our comms equipment, Closure?

lines.x: Already on it.
Rules are bound to be analyzed. There must be a source for this unusual shroud.
I'll just need a bit of time.
If we don't want the Nachzehrer cutting the battlefield to itty-bitty pieces in the fog, turning our offensive into more of a buffet...
We should fill the plaza with repeaters, including those curvy corridors on the sides...
We don't have time to get a bunch more equipment, but luckily, I have a backup plan!

lines.st: I never told them that I had never killed anyone. I did exterminate a few districts' worth of musbeasts, but never a human.
I never told them that it was not an Arts-imbued pall I wrapped myself in, but rags taken from stolen mops.
After all, I had never met another Nachzehrer in all the years I grew up in the ditches of Londinium, and no one ever taught me the Nachzehrer Arts.
I wrapped myself in mops so the Victorians wouldn't beat me to death. A 'Mop-Head' that killed musbeasts for them deserved to live more than the Sarkaz, at least.

lines.x: Er, why are you ripping the cloth off of yourselves?
Our Patriarch says we need no longer hide the decay.
Sh—Should I rip mine off too, then?
Of course, child.
Er... why doesn't it fly away like yours did?
Am I missing some Arts?
Feel the decay within you returning to the land. Feel the withered life returning to your body.
Then, rejoice.
So, I should rejoice...?

lines.st: I beat a Nachzehrer to a pulp.
They said the Nachzehrer could never be truly killed. So I kept swinging my wrench like my life depended on it, even though the one beneath me was no longer moving.
His ugly frame was much frailer than I imagined. I broke it like the fallen branches in the snow under my feet in the winters of my childhood.
They always broke with such a satisfying crack. Was the war answering my courage?
I liked the feeling.

lines.x: I could. I said I could.
Who said you can't truly kill a Nachzehrer? This one seems pretty dead to me!
Careful there! You dinnae want to be near a Nachzehrer corpse long!
The Nachzehrer warrior's body quickly collapses, losing all form and color.
An invisible fog takes up the form of the dead, and gently embraces the remains.
It just rots on its own? That quick?
If we dinnae get away now, we and that body both'll be Nachzehrer food.
Bloody hell, and there I was, thinking the Nachzehrer weren't such bloody monsters after all...
It'll be fine, alright! Where d'you come from? Think about home! All that sun and hay and beer, you know!
I think you'll feel a wee bit better puttin' all this rot out of your mind!
I'm from Londinium! All I had at home was sourdough hard enough to smash skulls!
Aye, that works!
Yeah, works for me!
Death and decay are defined by silence. I never thought the Sudaram could operate so noisily.
No longer do the Nachzehrer try to keep their nature in check. They are allowing decay to run unchecked.

opts: Meaning it'll be harder to stop the spread of rot?

lines.x: A pair of hands cannot stop rushing water.

opts: But they can easily spray the water further.
What if we 'diluted' the rot, Logos?

lines.x: Hang in there. You'll get better.
AAAARRRRRGGGGHHHHH!!! DO I LOOK LIKE I'LL GET BETTER TO YOU?!
A natural effect of rapidly applying healing Arts.
Take this, give it to—argh! Uh—
—The pain... it's getting better?
Do not choose the end out of pain, soldier. Endure.
The pain will stop, in time.
Thank you, Sarkaz...
Listen to Liz, it'll be alright. In cases of acute Infection, we have to avoid rapid emotional fluctuations. Take a deep breath, and—
Message from the Doctor, Nearl.

opts: Shining, Nearl, Nightingale, are you in the rear?

lines.x: We're at the rear camp. Just returned with a bunch of wounded.

opts: How is Nightingale?

lines.x: Weak, but she refused to abandon the injured to their fates. We'll take care of her.

opts: I need you to pull out of the area, along with everybody in camp.

lines.x: Doctor, you...
Let us know if you need any support.

opts: The Nachzehrer shroud won't be easily dispelled with Arts.
We'll divert the fog of decay to nearby areas.
Do your best to ensure that no one's caught in it.

lines.x: Roger.
...The more lives we save, the less decay there is to feed on.
It's the duty of the Followers, isn't it?
Nearl. Shining. There's no time to rest.
Liz...
Liz's blue fowlbeast perches on Shining's shoulder, rubbing against her cheek.
*Sigh* Sometimes I just don't know what to do with you.
Nearl?
Go and save some lives. As for the enemy—
Leave them to my spear and shield.
'Bzzz—bzzzz—'
'Bzzzt—'
Wow, I never knew fog could be so noisy!
The interference is too strong! Might be easier to yell than get a signal through!
We can't place the repeaters directly on the ground! The fog eats away at the antennae!
If we put up a shield...
No, with interference like this, that'd just block whatever's left of the signal.
What if we carried the repeaters?
The fog won't eat at the equipment as long as we're still alive, right?
Closure clenches her comms terminal tightly, but she can feel the pain of its metal corners biting into her skin fading away. Her limbs are becoming numb.
The fog clings to all life equally, inviting them to join the procession of decay.
She knows what Fang's suggestion implies. She wants to reject it.
But she does not.
You go. I'll stay here with the base station.
Think, Closure, think! If this fog is from Nachzehrer Arts, then that can't just be meaningless noise.
Identify the pattern, and we can filter it out!
Mm...
There it is! It's not noise, it's the voice of the Nachzehrer!
Even if their flesh has decayed, even if their consciousness has long faded, the lifeforce of the Nachzehrer continues to sing in joy as they return to the Cycle.
No idea what you're so happy about, but I'm sick of hearing it!
Lemme see... a little more over here...
'Bzzz—bzzzz—'
'Clo—sure—bzzz'
I'm getting a signal! But it's still not enough...
How about this?
That's what I'm talking about! Wait, Misery? How did you...?
I can cut a path through the fog for communications.
Great! Now we can hear what—
3rd Caster Battalion! Engage the Nachzehrer emerging from behind the lines!
Don't let them cut us off from the Exemplars!
The weight-bearing structures of the corridors to the sides of the plaza are heavily damaged! They're about to collapse!
Watch out for falling debris!
The whole battlefield...
...It's connected.

lines.st: Now I have killed. A Victorian girl wearing a bright printed scarf on her head.
Her scarf faded and her complexion twisted before my face. She became no different from the musbeasts I killed, only bigger and more hollow.
Her life must have flowed into me. I find myself swinging the greatsword that they gave me, almost as tall as I am.
But I don't feel the ecstasy they spoke of.

lines.x: AHHH—!
Phew—you, you're rotten too.
If I don't rot, you will, is that it?
Wh—What's that?
The massive explosion almost ruptures the young Nachzehrer's eardrums. A massive suit of armor flies past him.
The fog gently picks away the broken limbs the armor is wearing, pale fragments dispersing with the steam.
Several Sudarams fly towards it, their swords making screeching sounds on the metal, piercing the dull thud of Originium explosives.
(Sound of gushing steam)
Metal... shall... decay...
The young Nachzehrer has no time to look back. Heavy metal legs break the fog in front of him, blocking out the already-dim sunlight.
How many years do you think it took us to build The Crawler? You think we'd just let you eat it?
Armor plate to the portside foreleg!
Does the main gun still turn? Two o'clock, fire!
Blast a path through the Sudaram for the Steam Knight!
The young Nachzehrer falls to the ground. Cold blood immediately seeps through the remaining mop cloth, as though he was born to wipe such filth.
But why was he born to?
He has no time to think about the answer. A great iron hammer is raised above his head, its glaring reflection in his eyes.
No, no—
I don't want to—
You don't want to die.
A tide of rot rushes past him, and the hammer falls to the ground as scrap.
M—My Patriarch...?
I—I'm sorry, I'm not, I didn't...
Please, don't punish me... I'm not a deserter, I just—
If you have yet to fully comprehend war, then you are no deserter. It is not yours to give up your life so easily in.
Your war has yet to come.
Go.
The fog... it's fading.
More and more wounded are being evacuated before they die. The Nachzehrer are being deprived of the rot they feed on.
'Disperse.'
The fog is thinning.

opts: Even I can breathe now.

lines.x: Wait, you couldn't breathe all this time, Doctor?
Why didn't you say anything?

opts: It wasn't that bad. It's just better now.

lines.x: This won't last for long. Shining reports that the fog that had been diverted elsewhere is returning.
We must move—
Doctor—!
Mon3tr!
'Perish.'

lines.st: You feel a sense of deja vu.
It is not that any memories came back to you. It is that, once again, you sense how tiny and frail your life is.
A blade of grass in the wilderness. What is one blade or a thousand in the presence of death? You will all become nutrition for next year's sprouts.
The tide of rot crosses Amiya's black curtain, Mon3tr's massive frame, and Logos's wall of incantations, rushing towards you.
The next instant, it comes to an abrupt halt.

lines.x: It is you.

opts: It is me.
What?

lines.x: You have kept your promise.
Mon3tr, Structural Fortification! Protect the Doctor!
There is no need, Dame. I shall not fight you here.
Even though this is a battlefield?
Precisely because it is a battlefield.
You do not belong to this battlefield of mine. Every second you spend here is an insult to it.

opts: War doesn't belong to anyone. It only destroys everyone.

lines.x: Hah! How arrogant.
No matter. This war that has nurtured me, this war I have nurtured, is now over.
Even if it has cycled back and forth for a thousand years in the most uninteresting manner, even if the pain it gave rise to has become numbness, this war has achieved its goal.
Now, the Sarkaz are masters of their own destiny.
The Amnannam in Theresis's hands...
Theresis? No. The Amnannam is in the hands of all Sarkaz, scattered across the land for a thousand years.
From this war, they will come away with power, freedom, and a future.
What is left for me is the purest afterglow of war, one that knows no purpose but war itself.
If there is no purpose, then why do you—
Outblood child, so eager to put a stop to everything you do not wish to see.
If you see war as only evil, suffering as only a mistake, and sacrifice as only a waste, then you can bring neither destruction nor rebirth.
...Should I take this as the God of War advising the King of Sarkaz?
Very good! Finally, you call yourself king.
But it is not advice. My war may have won the Sarkaz a future, but it is not my place to dictate what it is.
My place is only to fulfill the very nature of my being.

opts: To spread decay?
To nurture new life?

lines.x: To me, decay and rebirth are one and the same.
As for you, ageless dame, outblood king, and you... It is not my place to judge you.
Go. Theresis awaits.
Behind the Nachzehrer King, the ranks of Sarkaz part silently.
They reveal a black path between white shroud, white bones, and white pall.
...Doctor.

opts: Only by taking down Theresis and Amnannam...
...will we truly bring an end to this calamity.

lines.x: We're with you, Doctor.
This is the moment Rhodes Island has prepared for. There is no need to hesitate.

opts: And... the Nachzehrer King?

lines.x: My whistle shall disperse the fog.
If we triumph, we will not celebrate victory alone.

lines.st: Through the fog, you can see the faint silhouette of a tank, the gushing steam of a Steam Knight, Exemplars and alliance soldiers fighting side-by-side—
You hear the communications of Closure, Wiš'adel, and the others—
All of them fighting for the same end...

opts: —An end to this war.
Let's go meet Theresis.

lines.x: You have crossed the waters of death, Aefanyl, and witnessed the dim candlelight of the other side.
Go. I do not wish to fight you.
Sing once more when all decay has returned to the soil, when all war drums are silent.
Your end does not lie here.
The Banshee shakes his head and says nothing. His bone pen floats up in front of him.
Hah. So be it.
...I do envy Laqeramaline.
The Nachzehrer King spreads his arms wide. The Banshee begins to move his bone pen.
A thousand branches rise from the Nachzehrer, formed of flesh, blood, and bone. A thousand incantations are etched by the point of the Banshee's pen, in opposition to the decay.

A thousand branches spread out on the battlefield. Every Nachzehrer corpse they touch, from the ethereal remains of the ancient Sudaram, to the yet-unrotted flesh of young Nachzehrer warriors, blooms in flowers of decay.
Once again, the Nachzehrer rise from decay.
Once again, the fearless charge forth in the incantations of the Banshee.

lines.st: The Palace of Westhaleg... It wasn't grand at all.
In fact, it looks even more decrepit than what I saw on that bill fifteen years ago.
The blood and flesh covering my hands feels more real. I stopped counting the number of Sarkaz I put down. It must've been a lot, because my wrench broke.

lines.x: I see him! That must be the Nachzehrer King!
Wait... This is as far as I can go.
*Cough* You can't charge in bare-handed. Take this sword.
Th—The Sighs of Kings—

lines.st: My daughter liked to rub my arm when I was lying face-down on the bed. She said it was fun because my muscles were so hard.
I would pat her shoulder and tell her she could become just as strong, strong enough to lift a whole pile of steel bars.
Holding the sword in my hand, I stab at the mountain of branches under me, and climb it.

lines.x: But how do I reach an enemy floating in mid-air?
(Quick puff of steam)
For Victoria!!!

lines.st: I see the Steam Knight charge towards death.
I hear a sharp whistle in my ear.
I feel the ground tremble from the guns of the tank...
The elusive, terrifying white figure in the sky stops for an instant. Then the Steam Knight dives from the sky, sending the Nachzehrer King crashing to the ground.

lines.x: He... He's so close...

lines.st: I am beginning to fall in love with war—I leap from the mountain of branches, and put the sword through the Nachzehrer King.
I can even feel my eyes burn at the heat gushing from the Steam Knight as it breathes.

lines.x: *Pant*... *pant*...
Victorian... you have learned to draw life from war.
Now, you must learn to return it to war.

lines.st: Hah. It was no use at all.
I am thrown a great distance. I can no longer feel my own body.
I see the Nachzehrer King once again rise into the sky, the Steam Knight wrapped in a frenzy of white cloth...
The strange Sarkaz casting his Arts, cutting a path towards the Nachzehrer King...
Familiar faces treading the death-filled path, towards that seemingly insurmountable foe with no hint of hesitation—
The fog seems to be dispersing. I see the massive four-legged tank advance... Why is there a golden-haired Kuranta here?
What are those strange contraptions that have appeared on the battlefield, making a strange sound?
I see Her Highness, our hero, Vina Victoria, the returned king, leading more and more people forward...
'For Victoria!'
I seem to see the end of the war.
The Nachzehrer King's silhouette overlaps with the sun. It is bright. Before him gathers those eager to end the war—
I can no longer see what happens next... where did the sword go?
Sorry, my girl... your daddy's not coming home.

lines.loc: Soul Furnace of KazdelEve of the Babel Exodus, 1086

lines.x: So you've chosen to answer Theresis's call to arms.
Even though the war will destroy you...
Has your crown shown you my end, Your Highness?
No. We simply understand full well that the only end of war is destruction.
What is there to fear in destruction?
The first Nachzehrer learned our purpose when he rose from the ashes of war.
'I am the end of all things.'
The great boulder of war sits above our heads on the slopes of time. All we can do is push it with all our might.
This time, however, Theresis has promised me an end to the cycle.
As had you. Do you remember?
I do, at the founding of the Military Commission.
But we have found another path.
Then prove it to me, Your Highness. I will wait.
Even if you do not find your end, however, I will carve out another path for the Sarkaz—
I can see your emotions. You are excited about this path.
I have waited too long, since that war two hundred years ago.
Farewell, Your Highness. I will witness the conclusion of your dispute with the Regent.
...Babel will return.
I have never doubted it. Nor have I doubted your promise.
Farewell.
The rays of dawn shine from the pinnacle of the Palace of Westhaleg.
They drive away the fog, revealing the aftermath of battle.
The fog is gone.
So is the Nachzehrer King.
Did we win? He's dead, right?
...Innit?
I still sense that suffocating presence.
...We'll need to get used to it. There is much to be done.
The daybreak was unfamiliar to those returning home, or to those on the battlefield.
Morning rays pour down like blood, over the carpet of bodies on the ground.
Is this what the sunrise always looked like in Londinium?
No... never.
The cold dawn pours blood toward the skyline, toward the winding mountains in the distance, like an ocean of blood.
The few cries of joy from the crowd quickly fall silent. This is not how anyone imagined the glory of victory.
It's over.
But who can truly kill war?
No one can kill war.
Do you still sense his presence, Your Highness?
He has unfortunately given himself to death. But you are correct. War will never end.
He merely left because this particular war is over.
I almost envy him.
He has taken so many people along with him, all to enjoy a war that belonged only to him.
I have lost another fine opponent.
Tch. Guess the old man really is dead.
No time to mull over it. The Victorians could begin their purge at any moment.
There's a lot on your mind.
There's just something I'm not sure about.
I have this feeling that, when the next war to change the history of Terra comes—
No, I'm probably overthinking it.
In the silence, only the lonely sound of a bone whistle reaches the pitch-black underground throne room.
It is the Banshee's elegy for the dead.
Is the Regent gone?
...Did you hear it? What is your true plan?
There is no answer. The Regent of Kazdel, the one who began this war, is not here.
He closes his eyes and seems to see a river of blood flowing towards the sky.
Manfred cannot even tell what became of the Sarkaz hero who left so many legends behind.
But he sees an image in his mind's eye—
When the fires of war once again ravage Kazdel, death shall emerge from the mountains on the skyline.

lines.loc: LifeboneOctober 1098, during the retreat of the Sarkaz forces

lines.x: Hoederer, I've brought along everyone who agreed to use the bones to retreat.
Most of them wouldn't be able to withstand the long journey—the old, the young, and more injured Sarkaz than you can count.
W said, and I quote, 'Let them die if they want,' but the other members of her squad carried them here.
Somehow, she still has quite a few living subordinates—enough to maintain order within the Lifebone and watch over Ulšulah in captivity.
So at least you won't have to worry about having no one to take the wheel if you accidentally die on the road.
...Got it.
What are you waiting for?
The Lifebone casts a shadow over the ruins of the battlefield.
None of the Sarkaz raise their heads. They may already know of—or have grown accustomed to—the greater shadow that will soon envelop their kilometers-long procession.
Most of them move slowly as they cross the trenches left behind by the high-speed battleships.
I was just thinking... Look how long the line of soldiers is.
We've all traveled that road from Kazdel to Victoria before. We all know how long it is.
But if the Victorians break their word and open fire on the retreating Sarkaz forces, there'll be nothing you can do about it.
Yes. That's how it's always been.
Maybe not immediately, but after the signal we send to all of Terra, every nation will be forced to respond.
Are you sure that will work?
Such a blatant retreat back to Kazdel will probably cause many countries to panic.
But there really is no better way.
Our squad must continue to present the strongest possible front to all factions. The moment we lose the strength to wield our weapons, we'll be devoured without a trace.
The same goes for the new 'King of Sarkaz'.
We must seize the initiative here.
The airship in the distance is faintly visible through the bones, and the ship's cannons remain locked in targeting mode.
A massive Catastrophe cloud still hovers around the ship, following the Sarkaz forces as they retreat.
But there's something else that's got me very worried.
Are you sure it's a good idea to have W guard Manfred?
She's the best for the job.
The squad has reached its destination.
The weather is clear, and visibility is good.
Keep looking to the southeast.
We've received the latest intelligence from the Ministry of Defense, which includes Kazdel's approximate coordinates.
Please closely monitor the Sarkaz army, to ensure their retreat home isn't a cover for another military maneuver.
Understood.
The officer does not, in fact, understand why he needs to monitor the Sarkaz.
However, out of respect for his comrades, he sits in the reconnaissance vehicle and stares intently at the data being sent back by the drones.
All is calm.
There's nothing unusual to report at the moment, sir. By the way—and I'm just making small talk—Maylander rarely sends someone of your status to liaise with the military.
Heh, the Victorians think they've done a fine job covering up the disaster in Londinium.
Maylander needs to investigate the situation before we have a talk with them.
Understood.
...Lieutenant! Lieutenant!
You are to report any new developments immediately!
Of course, sir. There's nothing to report right now.
Nothing at all?!
Wait. What's that...?
The officer puts down the communicator. The Catastrophe warning system on the vehicle is blaring an alarm.
He looks toward the capital district, but at this distance, he can barely make out the outline of the nomadic city.
But he can clearly see the massive shadow cast by the Catastrophe cloud, stretching from above the capital district all the way to where he stands.
Yes. He knows you are familiar with the people of Siracusa, so he hopes you'll have a chance to display your talents this time.
Not to worry. The Sarkaz hordes are a chaotic, calamitous force, but they are ultimately mercenaries.
We are now well aware that our neighbor, Victoria, has failed to keep them on a tight leash.
But I shall not allow such filth to disrupt Leithanien's glorious Güldenesgesatz. Of this I am quite certain.
Please send this message along to your master—
The noble is interrupted by a suddenly darkening sky.
...What's going on?
Come inside with me now, sir! It's a Catastrophe!
Contact the base for further orders... Damn! We've got no signal! What the hell is going on? Push the fleet forward. Full throttle!
Wait—
The data from the Catastrophe warning system sends a chill down his spine.
The cloud has stopped expanding, just as suddenly as it had appeared a few minutes ago.
The strong wind blows Originium dust up into the sky, but it doesn't fall back down.
Then the cloud starts to dissipate as the sunlight peaks through.
Can you hear me, lieutenant?
Yes, sir! The Catastrophe cloud appeared out of nowhere. There were no signs at all before it happened...
Of course, of course. This was meant as a display, a show of force to intimidate us.
You think the Sarkaz troops did this...?!
Maylander no longer requires your help with our investigation. Please withdraw at once. Of course, do not disclose any information about our cooperation.
...Yes sir.
May Terra bless us.
Enough. Deactivate the tip line. We have more than enough eyewitness accounts of the Catastrophe cloud!
Everyone saw what happened. Those of us living in the high rises got an even closer look!

For now, I need all of you to come up with explanations and file your stories. Everyone writes a report—whether you explain it away as an advertising hologram or new Arts from Leithanien, that's up to you.
...Do you need anything else?
Sir, this call wasn't from a citizen...
We just received word from our Messengers... Zwillingstürme, Laterano, Montelupe... Catastrophe clouds have appeared over the capitals of every nation.
What about Deity Grypherburg and Max D.C.?
...Same situation.
That little 'Amnannam' thingamajig is pretty handy. It can send a Catastrophe to someone's doorstep before you can blink.
Tch, no wonder Doctor and the hag were so nervous when we came to take it away, and the little bunny insisted on tagging along to keep an eye on me.
Hmm...What if we just, like, destroyed every capital of the Core all at once?
While it may indeed be able to rain down disaster on those nations, that doesn't mean it can destroy them.
I have no doubt that doing so would bring nothing but retaliation against Kazdel. Our history has repeatedly proven this, even from the earliest records.
It would be unwise, Wiš'adel. In fact, you must use Amnannam sparingly—the strain it places on the mind and body is even greater than I imagined.
You just experienced that pain firsthand.
You don't get to tell me that, Manfred.
Amnannam's potential extends far beyond intimidation, assuming we figure out the proper use for it.
Their Highnesses had much higher hopes for it. Yes, it is a weapon, a deterrent, but more importantly, it is the means by which we will break free from our destiny—
You know, you're even more annoying than Hoederer.
Don't forget who you are, dude. You're a prisoner, and we're only keeping you alive 'cause Kazdel needs you alive.
Hell, the only reason we let the Military Commission stick around is that the surviving Sarkaz still need something to believe in on the way home.
Fucking Theresis sure thought of everything, huh?
...I'm guessing Hoederer saw this coming long ago too, didn't he?
This was always the Regent's overt strategy: to reunite a people—brought together once more by hatred—around Kazdel—
Even Babel and the Military Commission are working together again, united toward a common goal.
Yeah yeah, but don't expect to wriggle your way out of this one, Manfred. Remember what I said.
Wiš'adel...
What, still got more to say?
If, someday, Kazdel really does normalize its relations with the other nations, just as Her Highness Theresa once strived for...
Then I, along with the rest of the Military Commission, will have to draw a clear line between ourselves and Kazdel.
As the Sarkaz see it, outbloods bring only pain and hatred, just as we do to them.
Sooner or later, they will claim that we haven't paid for the crimes we have committed, and on that day I will be waiting for those hateful avengers to find us—
Just shut up already. Stop acting like you're a saint or whatever.
Theresis and the rest of you assholes screwed up big time. You were so goddamned stupid I wish I could blow you to pieces right now.
Don't forget: almost all the lunatics who actually believed in your plan died in Londinium, along with the Nachzehrer.
You killed them with your own hands. There were hardly any left to even evacuate.
But the Sarkaz will pay for their own mistakes.
Babel's not so spineless as to cast the entire Commission from the city just because a few dumbasses were bold enough to provoke Kazdel.
Her Highness would never do that, and neither will I.
If they're stupid enough to come, I don't mind giving 'em a fight.
But if you keep talking shit, I'll just toss you over to someone who's willing to put up with your crap.
You're not afraid of it?
Look at that moving Catastrophe cloud over there. It seems so far away, but I can't shake the feeling it might suddenly appear right above my head...
Of course I'm afraid. Who isn't afraid of Catastrophes?

But there's a trick I picked up in Sami: whenever I'm scared, I just pull my hat over my head, crouch down, and think of something happy. The things you're afraid of will disappear before you even know it.
After all, I'm just minding my own business—they wouldn't give me a hard time for no reason, would they?
Just last week you said you'd lost count of how many times you'd been captured and interrogated by one of the Famiglie.
*sigh* I'm sorry for the trouble.
But misunderstandings always get cleared up in the end. I mean, you guys did let me go, didn't you?
...I really can't figure out how you managed to make it all the way through Sami and Ursus in one piece. You ain't lying about that too, are ya?
I just happen to come across perfectly reasonable people like you all the time.
Hmph. Try reason your way out of Catastrophes, then.
Remind me again where you're going? Some place called 'Black Flow'?
Yes, and I'm also going home, friend, back home to Bolívar.
That's one long-ass journey. An extra day or two won't make a difference. Besides, that tattered paper talisman you're carrying ain't gonna expire.
Anyhow, I advise you to wait for that parade of crazies to pass. They started a war; they can destroy cities.
Or maybe wait for someone or something else to test out the Catastrophe for you.
Just some friendly advice.
Those devils are bold as hell—someone's bound to try and take a bite out of 'em sooner or later.
He can hear the terrifying roar of the Catastrophe cloud off in the distance, along with the faint rumble of battleship engines.
The Siracusan is right. It may be wiser not to get involved in this, however...
Paryaqaqa, you ain't actually... fascinated by that cloud, are ya?
Maybe I am. I find it hard to hide my excitement whenever I encounter something I've never seen before.
True, I paid a price for it back in Sami, but I just can't get rid of this bad habit.
To be more precise, I want to take a look at the people walking beneath the Catastrophe cloud.
How is it going? I hope no one is giving you any trouble.
Just another ordinary funeral.
...A funeral beneath a Catastrophe cloud is hardly ordinary. It's unfair to so many—they should have been able to return to Kazdel with us.
Death is the one form of equality most readily attainable to all, Misery.
The vast majority of the deceased have no relatives, nor even names.
When I asked them to leave their final words, they could not even identify anyone who might remember them after they were gone.
I shrouded the burial site in mist to keep beasts away. Unfortunately, most of the dead were still unable to return to the furnace at home as they wished.
Logos, do you know what lies a little further up ahead?
My previous missions... rarely required me to come this way.
Laterano is just south of here, not far.
When Outcast and I went on missions together in this area, she'd often bring me here to drink.
We once made a foolish bet—that I'd find a way to sneak in and try Lateran ice cream.
Well, I did it. No one caught me.
But I never got the chance to tell her.
Ha. At least I'm not the one breaking my promise this time.
So you deliberately refused the order to return to the ship with Ascalon.
You knew we'd pass through here during the retreat.
Don't try to guess my intentions, Logos.
With Ascalon here, we'll have no issues escorting our fallen comrades and their tokens back to the ship.
Besides... she also needs some time to herself. To say goodbye to the past.
I tried speaking to her about the retreat, but she remained silent.
Perhaps she won't have anything to say about what happens next, either.
She witnessed the end of an era, Logos. It couldn't have been easy for her, and I fear we'll have one less participant at our future meetings.
Misery lets out another sigh.
No one can say for sure what will happen next.

It's just like the Catastrophe cloud above our heads... I may not like this plan, but I must admit it's the best deterrent we have against those who wish to seize the chance to declare war on the Sarkaz.
Without a strong measure like that to avoid war, there would only be more unnecessary sacrifices on the road back to Kazdel.
Violence is a means, never an end. There is nothing wrong with that.
Maybe I'm just a pessimist...
But I still think the rain will come down, sooner or later.
Uh-huh. So can you let me go?
Who the hell are you, anyway?
I'm really just an explorer.
What kinda explorer could stop the lightning in a Catastrophe cloud? I-It's like the thunderbolts themselves are afraid of you...
Maybe they've taken a liking to me?
See? A few of them just landed right next to me!
You do realize that just makes you even more suspicious, right?
Personally, I think the Catastrophe's sending a clear message—don't come near us, and don't try and test us!
Now now, let's not be hasty. I just wanted to study the strange Catastrophe cloud you created, as well as remind you that—
I don't like you.
You tell too many lies.
The Sarkaz mercenary sizes up the man standing before him, and hesitates about whether to make a move.
This guy's the one you were talking about?
He looks pretty normal to me.
You sure you got this right, old geezer?
He is giving off a particular scent, one I loathe.
(Ancient Sarkaz language) 'Šmal'deqa'... 'enemies from outside'.
No... It is not coming from him, it is what he brought with him.
Strange. It seems to be... a piece of paper?
Pfft. You really are confused, you old coot.
So does this mean we don't need to worry about him anymore? Hey! Why aren't you saying anything?
Boss, I—
I told you, call me 'Chairman' from now on. Tch, what a pain in the ass. Why did Hoederer have to make up all these stupid rules?
Only Her Highness should be called 'Chairman'...
Anyway, your squad's out here to scout, so just keep on scouting. Leave this guy to me.
Got it.
You. If you've got something to say, say it. Don't try and pull a fast one on me.
I guarantee I'm faster than you.
There's a fleet heading your way. Don't know which nation they're from—they've disabled all identifying signals.
They're coming from that direction. The terrain provides a lot of cover. It would be easy to plan an ambush.
I guess everyone wants in on this.

Tch, what a bunch of cowards. Afraid of us, yet always acting cocky.
Alright, I'll pass on the word to the guys charging to their deaths on the frontlines
—Hello? Can you hear me? While you're out there, watch out for an ambush.
So as a thank-you, how about I invite you to watch the Catastrophe cloud with me? You're here to 'explore', right?
Hahaha... I'd really rather not. I'm shaking in my boots here.
Hmph.
Alright. Your loss. Now scram. If any clueless Sarkaz grabs you again, just tell them who let you go—Wiš'adel.
You have my thanks.
That sure was a close call. If you hadn't rescued me, this journey would have been my last.
Drop the act around me. I know you got caught on purpose.
It's so weird. Why do I keep running into people who have nothing to do with the Sarkaz, but want to help anyway?
The Liberi eyes the seemingly relaxed Sarkaz before him, quickly realizing she's likely the leader of the group—and that her attention has long since shifted away from him.
She is focused on the distant horizon. She is heeding his warning.
The wind brings with it the sounds of the land... or maybe the rumbling of ships' engines.
Conflicts, wars... the explorer has already seen too much along the way.
*sigh*...

lines.st: 'As always, we don't want to be the ones waiting for a beating.'
'All the Arts creations we sent out have been destroyed. They know we're getting closer, but I never planned on hiding anyway.'
'Some unlucky souls have to be sent to their deaths so the others can board the ship and fight. Will it be your squad or mine?'
'Follow the orders of Babel and the Military Commission—this isn't for you to decide on your own.'
'Kill every hostile you encounter. Anyone in the squad who spares an enemy or takes spoils from their comrades—kill them too.'
'Prepare the explosives. Set up altars along the route.'
'Normally, the altars' Arts can't penetrate the armor of high-speed battleships. But this time, we have the Catastrophe cloud on our side.'

lines.x: They're here.
I knew going home wouldn't be that easy.
The earth is shaking.
The high-speed battleships slowly emerge from the shadows, blocking the Sarkaz's path forward.
The warrior launched a few charges like this while in Londinium, and considers himself one of the fortunate few.
He also knows full well that the path back to Kazdel is not a road, but a battlefield, full of corpses and awash in blood.
The Sarkaz procession stops at the edge of the firing range of the battleships' cannons.
The sky grows dark, as the black cloud overhead lets loose with rolling thunder. Their enemy is approaching.
Everyone, commence preparations like you were ordered.
Each of you just do your own job.
I don't want to catch any idiots of you showing off—

lines.st: 'Halt.'
'You may not advance any further.'

lines.x: Only a few Sarkaz have noticed the young Cautus girl within their ranks.
She marches towards the head of the line.
Then, with a wave of her staff, the girl draws a line behind where she stands. The black line spreads across the earth.
A black wall rises up where the line once was.
An impregnable wall, woven of black silk threads, separates the two opposing forces.
The Sarkaz can no longer see the Cautus girl on the other side of the wall.
But one thing remains imprinted in their memories—the black crown floating above her head.
The King of Sarkaz.
Their king has not abandoned them? They have a new king?
But... why an outblood king?
The new king's voice resonates in the minds of every Sarkaz present. Meanwhile, the fleet sent to block them clearly hears her words:
The Sarkaz are going home.
I will not rule out violence if necessary. I know full well the weight of this Black Crown.
Theresa has already led the myriad souls away. For the first time, the Sarkaz have a choice.
I will not let her efforts be in vain.
Nor do I want the Sarkaz, or any of you, to start another war.
The cycle of hatred and revenge must end.
This is the first time Amiya has openly revealed her identity since Theresa taught her all there was to know about the Black Crown inside the Assimilated Universe.
Now she can hear noisy cries from amidst the Sarkaz.

lines.st: 'Impossible! It can't be!'
'An outblood stole the Sarkaz's crown? How?! Why?!'
'She is our king? Do I have to make a tribute? What does she want from us?'
'Let's go! It's onto the next battlefield!'

lines.x: Terror, suspicion, reverence, ecstasy. A wave of emotions floods Amiya's mind.
In the past, she has made several attempts to explore the information stored within the Black Crown, but she has never gone this far.
The tide of information nearly overwhelms her. For millennia, the Sarkaz have desperately sought their 'King', chasing an elusive vision.
She tries her best to steady her thoughts and maintain the unshakable black wall.
I am no ruler, nor am I the King of Sarkaz.
If the Sarkaz hate me for this, I will accept that.
I will show them the way forward, the path to end their suffering.
Both sides should stand down.
There won't be any more war.
The black wall still stands, just as the Catastrophe swirls above the wilderness.
After a long stalemate, the first battleship in the fleet begins to change direction.
Great. Finally...
The wave of thoughts suddenly fade away, and she hears an unusually clear voice—

lines.st: '...Who are you?'

lines.x: Remain silent.
As His Holiness commanded.
Even though the Sarkaz have committed countless crimes within our holy city? Even though Laterano has never before faced such provocation and desecration?
Even though their army is openly threatening the safety of our residents? Even then, we will not respond?
Yes. His Holiness believes that Laterano should not interfere in this matter.
I wish to see him. I'm going to file a protest!
...We are unsure where he is right now.
The sudden disturbance of the halo, and the flood of information...
For whatever reason, it suddenly became aware of something.
'A conversation'.
It is... having a conversation. With something similar to itself.
Since it has decided to make its choice, I am afraid I must follow suit. The new revelation it has showed us is something Laterano can no longer bear on its own.
Let me think... let's start with the first question.
...Who are you?

lines.st: 'I have to admit, Your Holiness, that this is a long road.'
'Even without the whispers of the myriad souls trapped in Originium for millennia, it will still take a long time for the Sarkaz and this land to let go of their hatred.'
'Well then, Amiya, my child, what if we do not have that much time?'
'What if Terra is no longer in its infancy and has come to the point where it has to face its future—'
'Theresa warned me this might happen.'

lines.x: Someone walks into the static void.
He is following someone else's footsteps, although their footprints have vanished from this place forever.
The tower is still collapsing, a hint at everything that has transpired.
Although time has no meaning here, he must admit that he's a step late.
She is already completely out of reach.
Now all he can see is the golden ocean and the lonely diamond in the sky.

lines.st: 'Amiya, not even I, the pope, know when or where the disaster will occur.'
'But I am certain that it will be a crisis that we've never faced before—'
'It is right before our eyes.'
